(viii) Need to establish a cooperative bank in Chikkaballapur
district, Karnataka
DR. K. SUDHAKAR (CHIKKBALLAPUR): Chikkaballapur district has a
substantial agrarian community, making it imperative to establish a dedicated
District Cooperative Bank to enhance agricultural credit flow. Given the district’s
crucial role in supplying Bengaluru and the surrounding regions, such a bank
would serve as a catalyst for rural economic growth, significantly benefiting local
farmers and agricultural productivity. A specialized cooperative bank focused on
agrarian credit would operate democratically, ensuring accessible and affordable
financial support tailored to farmers’ needs. This would not only strengthen
Chikkaballapur’s agricultural sector but also have a positive spillover effect on
neighboring districts. I urge the Government of India to consider this proposal,
recognizing its potential to empower farmers and drive sustainable rural
development.
(ix) Need for a comprehensive plan for pilgrimage circuit by connecting all
religious sites of the Jharkhand
DR. NISHIKANT DUBEY (GODDA): The Ministry of tourism has included
Deoghar city (Jharkhand) in the PRASAD Yojana. At the time of its inclusion, I
had raised an issue concerning the narrow streets of the city leading to the
temple and suggested for widening of these streets. The Prime Minister in the
year 2015 had announced a package for the development of religious sites in
the region, especially the Sultanganj to Deoghar corridor for the betterment of
the facilities in the Kanwar Yatra and the overall holistic development of
24.03.2025 941
religious sites in the area. The Deoghar, Santhal Pargana, and Jharkhand
region is the hub of numerous religions, including Jainism and Hinduism. A
comprehensive plan should be chalked out for the development of a circuit
which connects the major religious hubs of the pious region. This circuit will
impact India’s reputation globally. Parasnath, Deoghar, Basukinath, Maluti,
Tarapith, Vikramshila, Bateshwarsthan, Mandar, Champapuri, sultanganj,
Karangari in Munger are all pivotal religious hubs with hundreds of millions of
devotees flocking to these temples annually to pay their respects. Thus, I
request to complete the projects for the development of the backward region.
(x) Need to establish multi-temperature air cargo facilities in all the airports
of Andhra Pradesh
SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI (RAJAHMUNDRY): The Finance
Minister in her Union Budget 2025 speech has announced prioritizing air cargo
infrastructure development, especially for high-value perishable horticulture
produce. However, I want to highlight the urgent need for multi-temperature air
cargo facilities in Andhra Pradesh's airports, which currently lack such
infrastructure. Despite being a major exporter of horticulture produce, seafood,
pharmaceuticals, and textiles, Andhra Pradesh does not have a single airport
equipped with modern air cargo warehousing and temperature-controlled storage.
Due to this gap, exporters are forced to transport goods by road to airports in
neighboring states, leading to higher logistics costs, increased transit times, and
quality deterioration of perishable commodities. I request the Government to
immediately initiate the development of air cargo facilities at Vijayawada,
24.03.2025 942
Rajahmundry, Tirupati, and the upcoming Bhogapuram Airport (Visakhapatnam)
under the Airports Authority of India (AAI). I request the Minister of Civil Aviation
to direct the preparation of a Detailed Project Report (DPR) for this infrastructure,
ensuring seamless air cargo movement from Andhra Pradesh to global markets.
This initiative will enhance exports, create jobs, and boost economic growth in the
region. I urge the Government to take urgent action to establish world-class air
cargo facilities in Andhra Pradesh.

(xv) Need for cement concrete paving of dilapidated rural roads constructed
under PMGSY in Gadchiroli Parliamentary Constituency, Maharashtra
DR. NAMDEO KIRSAN (GADCHIROLI-CHIMUR): I would like to bring to your
attention that the Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana (PMGSY) was launched by
the Central Government in 2000 to develop rural roads. Under this scheme,
asphalt roads were constructed from 2000 to 2014. Currently, due to inadequate
maintenance and repairs, these roads have deteriorated significantly. Asphalt
roads typically last only 3 to 5 years, and in high-rainfall regions, they degrade
even faster. To ensure long-term durability and better road infrastructure, these
roads must be upgraded with cement concrete paving. Therefore, I request the
Hon'ble Minister for the approval for the cement concrete paving of deteriorated
rural roads constructed under the PMGSY (Core Network) between 2000 and
24.03.2025 945
2014 in Gadchiroli Parliamentary Constituency.

(xx) Regarding reopening of JESSOP and Bengal Immunity Company
Limited in West Bengal
PROF. SOUGATA RAY (DUM DUM): I would like to raise an issue regarding
continued closure of JESSOP and Bengal Immunity in Dum Dum, Kolkata. The
JESSOP & Company was taken over by Government of India in the 70’s but
Government of India sold the company to Ruia Group in 2001. But the Ruia’s
failed to run the company and the company has remained closed for the last ten
years. The Government of West Bengal took over the company in 2016 and
decided to pay Rs. 10,000/- to every worker. But the Central Government has
refused to give permission for the State Government’s take over. As a result, the
company remains closed while the State Government pays the remuneration. I
demand that the Central Government give permission for the takeover. Bengal
Immunity which was a company under Govt. of India remains closed for more
than fifteen years. The Centre transferred the ownership of the company to
Bengal Chemicals. Its plea of not opening the company that there is a case in
High Court is not tenable. I demand that the Centre moves for opening the
company.

Electors Photo Identity Card (EPIC) numbers
SHRI KIRTI AZAD (BARDHAMAN-DURGAPUR): I wish to draw the attention of
the House to a matter of grave concern regarding the duplication of Electors
Photo Identity Card (EPIC) numbers. The integrity of the electoral process
depends on the uniqueness of voter identification, and any duplication poses a
24.03.2025 948
severe threat to free and fair elections. The Election Commission of India (ECI)
mandates that EPIC numbers be unique, with the first three letters indicating the
Assembly constituency. However, recent reports indicate that duplicate EPICs
exist across states, suggesting a systemic failure rather than isolated
administrative lapses. Such anomalies could lead to disenfranchisement,
undermining citizens' constitutional right to vote. The ECI must provide precise
data on the number of duplicate EPICs and clarify the mechanisms that allowed
these duplications. Given modern database capabilities, it must also explain why
safeguards to prevent duplication have failed.
A thorough and transparent investigation is imperative to restore public
confidence in the electoral process and uphold the credibility of our democracy. I
urge the Government to take immediate action in this regard.
(xxii) Need to review the decision for implementation of two-shift format for
National Eligibility Cum Entrance Test for Post-Graduate (NEET-PG)
Examinations
SHRI TAMILSELVAN THANGA (THENI): I wish to bring to your attention a
matter of urgent public importance concerning the conduct of the National
Eligibility cum Entrance Test for Postgraduate Medical Course (NEET-PG). The
National Board of Examinations in Medical Sciences (NBEMS) has adopted a
two-shift format with distinct question papers for NEET-PG, a practice that raises
serious concerns regarding fairness, equity, and the overall integrity of this crucial
examination that shapes the future of our medical professionals. In 2024, the
examination was held in two shifts (9:00 AM to 12:30 PM and 3:30 PM to 7:00
24.03.2025 949
PM) across 416 centers, accommodating 2,28,540 candidates. There is
substantial evidence indicating significant variations in the difficulty levels of
question papers across different shifts. This inherent disparity creates an uneven
playing field, leading to an unfair evaluation process. Candidates from the 2024
examination have reported instances where individuals with identical raw scores
received drastically different percentiles and ranks solely based on the shift they
were assigned. This undermines the principle of equal opportunity. While NBEMS
employs an AIIMS normalization process to standardize scores, methodology
lacks transparency and has been associated with inconsistencies, further eroding
the confidence of candidates in the fairness of the evaluation. This normalization
process, as implemented for NEET-PG, involves converting the raw scores of
candidates in each shift to a percentile score. The highest score achieved in each
shift is set to 100th percentile within that shift, regardless of the actual percentage
of marks. Scores of other candidates are then converted to percentiles based on
their relative performance within their respective shift. For example, if the top
score in Shift 1 is 80% and in 2 is 82%, both these scores would be normalized to
the 100th percentile for respective shifts. While this aims to put top performers on
equal footing, the conversion of intermediate scores to percentiles has been
inconsistent in addressing the fundamental disparities between shifts. The current
two-shift system undermines the fundamental principles of meritocracy and equal
opportunity that should be the bedrock of our examination system. Therefore,
there is an urgent need to reconsider the implementation of two-shift examination
policy for NEET-PG.
24.03.2025 950
(xxiii) Need for establishment of Tertiary Cancer Care Centre in
Andhra Pradesh
SHRI MAGUNTA SREENIVASULU REDDY (ONGOLE): I rise to highlight the
critical issue of the absence of a Tertiary Cancer Care Centre (TCCC) in the state
of Andhra Pradesh. Cancer is a major health challenge, and many citizens in our
state are unfortunately affected by it, putting their lives at significant risk. To
address the rising number of cancer cases, the NDA alliance in Andhra Pradesh
is proactively working to provide free cancer prevention services through
Government hospitals, ensuring that over 40 million people benefit from early
diagnosis and preventive care. However, prevention alone is not enough.
Strengthening our cancer treatment infrastructure is equally important, for which a
TCCC is vital. Andhra Pradesh, despite being one of the top 10 states in India
with the highest incidence of cancer, currently lacks such a centre. The absence
of a TCCC in our state severely hampers the ability of healthcare providers to
offer specialized, comprehensive care to cancer patients. Therefore, I would like
to request the Hon’ble Minister of Health and Family Welfare to establish a
Tertiary Cancer Care Centre in Andhra Pradesh. This facility will ensure that
cancer patients have access to specialized treatment, improving survival rates
and a better quality of life for those battling cancer.

(xxv) Need to expedite the release of Central share under various schemes
meant for tribal development in Maharashtra
SHRI RAJABHAU PARAG PRAKASH WAJE (NASHIK): I wish to draw the
attention of the Government to the urgent need for increasing the authorized
share capital of Shabari Tribal Finance and Development Corporation from ₹200
crore to ₹500 crore to effectively implement various Government-funded tribal
development programs in Maharashtra. This increase in capital is crucial for
supporting key welfare schemes, included Special Central Assistance (SCA) for
livelihood development of tribal communities, Article 275(1) scheme for Farmer
Producer Organization (FPO) formation and skill development and NSTFDC and
State schemes promoting entrepreneurship and employment generation for tribal
youth. As per the funding structure, 51% of the required capital is to be
contributed by the Maharashtra State Government, while 49% is to be provided
by the Central Government. However, the Central Government’s share of ₹245
24.03.2025 952
crore is still pending, delaying the effective implementation of these schemes.
Given the importance of these programs for tribal welfare, economic
empowerment, and self-sufficiency, I urge the Government to expedite the
release of the Central share at the earliest.
(xxvi) Need for publication of the report on excavation undertaken at
Keezhadi in Sivaganga district, Tamil Nadu
DR. D. RAVI KUMAR (VILUPPURAM): From 2013 to 2016 an ASI archaeologist
led the excavation at Keezhadi, Sivaganga district, Tamil Nadu, unearthing over
5,000 artifacts of historical significance. The archaeologist had already submitted
reports on the first and second phases of excavation to the Union Government,
Radiocarbon analysis revealed that the artifacts were approximately 2,600 years
old. The Tamil Nadu Government conducted subsequent excavations (Phases 4–
9) and published reports on them. However, the ASI has yet to release the report
on the first two phases of excavation. The 982-page report prepared by the
archaeologist holds crucial historical insights. Consequently, a case was filed in
the Madurai Bench of the Madras High Court, seeking a directive for the Union
Government to publish the report. During the proceedings, the Government’s
counsel assured the Court that the report would be released within nine months.
Accepting this, the Court, in its judgment on 27 February, 2024, directed the
Union Government to publish the Keezhadi excavation reports within nine
months. The deadline lapsed in November 2024, yet the report remains
unpublished. This constitutes contempt of Court. In light of the above, I urge the
immediate publication of the Keezhadi excavation report without further delay.
24.03.2025 953

DR. SHASHI THAROOR (THIRUVANANTHAPURAM): Madam Chairperson, a
few years ago, I was constrained to observe of my absent friend, the hon.
Finance Minister, that her Budget speech in this House reminded me of a garage
mechanic who said, “I could not fix your brakes, so I made the horn louder”.
Looking at this year's Finance Bill, in her absence, I think she has slightly
changed her tune. She is now saying to taxpayers, “I could not repair the roof, so
I brought you an umbrella”. This Finance Bill is a classic case of patchwork
solution at a time when the nation needs clarity, conviction and decisive
leadership.
The Government's economic management finds itself in the grasp of deep-
rooted structural challenges. We have seen growth targets being scaled down.
Double-digit growth is apparently unattainable, and the ambitions of maintaining a
respectable growth rate of even six per cent and above are fading away. The
portion of our population engaged in agriculture is higher than ever while
manufacturing has shrunk to around 15 per cent of GDP; the lowest in the
century. Our informal sectors of employment are soaring and dependence on
foreign capital inflows to manage the current account deficit is growing amidst the
staggering situation where our exports are stagnating and production is not going
up. Even those who are earning five or six times of per capita income are now
struggling to maintain their standard of living. So, ‘Viksit Bharat’ by 2047 is a
laudable objective for a quarter century from now but how does this Finance Bill
24.03.2025 957
begin to get us there?
It has taken the Government years to finally realize that just two per cent of
Indians, the hard-working taxpayers, have been really carrying this country on
their backs. Individual taxpayers, mainly the salaried middle class, were already
contributing more than the corporations last year and yet no action was taken.
That would have been the time to act as I pointed out in the last year's budget
debate, but now in the current financial year, corporate taxes have crawled up by
eight per cent while individual and non-corporate taxation has soared by 21 per
cent. Now, the Government finally wakes up and gives a sort of a tax break to the
salaried middle-class. Who else is financing the Government? The aam aadmi is
financing the Government through indirect taxes, most notoriously GST.
Instead of the good and simple tax that we all wanted, India has multiple
and confusing GST rates, including the highest GST rate in the world at 28 per
cent but the tax revenue still lags at 18 per cent of GDP. In contrast, China has 13
per cent GST cap but they collect 20 per cent of GDP with it. Vietnam has an
even lower eight per cent cap and collects 19 per cent of GDP. In Thailand, the
GST is only seven per cent and they get net 17 per cent of GDP.
Beyond exorbitant rates, our system carries a dubious tax – the dubious
burden of being the most complex tax in the world. Seventy-seven countries have
GST and they impose only one or two tax slabs. This multi-rate structure in our
country has only further raised the compliance burden for businesses.
Indeed, bifurcating input tax claims based on different product tax rates is a
significant hurdle for businesses. There is supposed to be an advanced ruling
24.03.2025 958
mechanism that should help, but I can tell you, from the food sector MSMEs in
Kerala, that their applications are left unheard for months, even years. Our
traditional snacks in Kerala, like Pazhampori, Vada, Ada, and Kozhakatta, face an
unfair 18 per cent tax simply because of the absence of an assignable HSN code.
With a shelf life of 124 hours, these snacks often go unsold and are discarded,
but sadly, their input tax claims are denied, leading to mounting losses. This
system, I am sorry to say, is less a tax and more a punishment, so much for the
claims of ease of doing business.
And where does all this money, all this tax, direct and indirect, end up?
Mostly lost, apparently, because Rs.28.59 lakh crore, both disputed and
undisputed, in unrealized tax revenue has been sitting untouched in this Finance
Bill, nearly double the amount from fiscal year 2021. Imagine if this was actually
realized, our entire fiscal deficit for fiscal year 2025 could be vanishing overnight.
Instead, Rs.13.97 lakh crore in corporate tax remains unrealized. Only Rs.9.11
lakh crore has been collected. Similarly, the non-corporate tax demand stands at
Rs.14.62 lakh crore. Collections lag at Rs.10.45 lakh crore. GST evasion has
doubled to Rs.2 lakh crore already this year, with 18,000 fake entities caught
while many more remain undetected.
Now, while all this is going on, between 2017 and 2022, 35,000 wealthy
Indians, the so-called high-net-worth individuals, left our country to relocate
abroad, complaining of tax terrorism. Obviously, when rich people migrate, their
wealth also migrates with them. This affects revenue generation, wealth creation
and employment opportunities. Surely, we need to review not just their effective
24.03.2025 959
tax rates, but the manner in which tax demands are made that drives such people
away. Unleashing tax terrorism is self-destructive, Madam. It reduces sources of
long-term revenue from future wealth creation.
The Government's attempts to raise money through disinvestment have
failed with underwhelming results. They have abandoned the strategy. Reports
suggest that the PLI scheme, introduced with such fanfare, will also be allowed to
lapse because it has not worked. Such is the state of affairs that the Government
now relies on the Reserve Bank of India's dividend as a major source of revenue.
That is neither sustainable nor ideal.
This Bill prompts another question, Madam Chairperson. If the Government
is set to forego almost Rs. One lakh crore in direct tax revenue and Rs.2,600
crore in indirect taxes on top of the inevitable drop in import tariffs due to likely
reductions in customs revenue prompted by our responses to the American
Government’s policies, and on top of that, declining capital gains tax amid a stock
market downturn, then the revenue receipts are going to be further squeezed
across all these categories. How can the Government claim it will cut the fiscal
deficit to 4.4 per cent for the coming financial year? The maths do not add up.
Welcome, Madam Finance Minister. Are you offering us jadugar
economics? How can we reduce the fiscal deficit in these circumstances and if
we do, who pays the price? And let me ask, Madam, very frankly: why is it that
only two per cent Indians are paying income tax? The reasons are endless -
unemployment, income inequality, poverty, inflation etc. But can the Government
continue to run itself on the backs of just two per cent of our people? While the
24.03.2025 960
economy is crawling forward at a snail's pace, income distribution is as lopsided
as ever. The share of the top one per cent more than doubled from 10 per cent in
1991 to 23 per cent in 2023. The top 10 per cent went from 35 per cent to 59 per
cent. The share of bottom 50 per cent of our population in our national income
has shrunk from 20 per cent to 13 per cent. Clearly, prosperity and growth are not
slow for everyone, just for those at the bottom half of our population. But they are
the ones who need progress the most.
Madam Chairperson, it is becoming almost painfully repetitive - every time
we talk about unemployment, we are faced with the same grim statistics. Reports
predict we need to generate 10 million jobs annually
from 2024 to 2030 to keep up with the growth targets, yet the skill mismatch is still
glaring. Only half of graduates of our country possess the skills required by
industries, leaving a significant gap in their employability, and skill development,
sadly, has been a failure. Just look at their placement figures.
While unemployment is a concern, the assessment of poverty is troubling
for a different reason. The NFHS-5 data reveals that about 15 per cent of India's
population is multi-dimensionally poor. Shockingly, the Government went 11
years without any consumption expenditure survey, leaving a significant void in
assessing poverty levels until it was finally conducted in 2024. But here is the real
issue. Our calculation of poverty levels is still based on the 2011 Census,
especially the sampling frame. An outdated dataset, completely unfit for
assessing the present socio-economic reality is what leaves the Government to
say, they have defeated poverty.
24.03.2025 961
According to the World Bank, the international extreme poverty line is USD
2.15 per person daily. They use a higher poverty line of USD 3.65 for the lower
middle-income countries, but India’s medium poverty line is only 1/10th of the
World Bank figure at the present exchange rate, which should make us wonder if
we truly understand the scale of poverty around us.
It is high time we acknowledge that the true extent of our poverty is very
grave, and we must adopt more accurate assessments to ensure that our efforts
at poverty alleviation really work, and have a lasting impact on the millions who
continue to struggle in silence.
The hopes of economic growth and prosperity seem to be slipping further
away as unemployment rises and poverty deepens. Jobs are scarce, wages are
stagnant, and just when struggling households think they might catch a break,
instead up easing the burden, small businesses are drowning in compliance
rules, informal workers are still making up a chunk of the workforce and they are
pushed further to the margins.
Since the Finance Minister has been kind enough to join us, let me capture
the present situation in some light verse.
Tax our petrol, tax our shirt,
Tax our footwear, tax our dirt,
Tax our mobile, tax our calls,
Tax our hovel's crumbling walls.
Tax our salary, tax our ride,
Tax our mithai on the side,
24.03.2025 962
Tax our sorrow, tax our cheer,
Tax our future, year by year.
GST, income, tax forms pile high,
Cesses, surcharges, make us sigh.
Roads with potholes, trains that derail,
“Where does it go?” Is it all a fail?
When we ask, "Is this all in vain?"
You smile and say, "It's for the nation's gain!"
And if we ask you to try and fix it,
You point and say, "you will be Viksit!"
Yet still we pay, with a patriotic heart,
Hoping our cash will play a small part,
In building a future, bright and fair,
For the nation we love, beyond compare!
Madam, let me say that the five Southern States, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil
Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka and Telangana, are among India's strongest economic
engines. They are paying taxes. They generate over a quarter of the country's
direct taxes. They contribute 28.5 per cent of GST. But when it comes to
receiving their fair share, they get a mere 15 per cent of the Centre's tax pool.
How is this acceptable? How can States that fuel the nation's economy be
expected to sustain their growth with such an inadequate return? By all means,
let us ask the richer States to help the poorer ones, but surely not in such a
disproportionate manner.
24.03.2025 963
The Finance Bill 2025 is yet another move by the Centre to hoard revenue
at the States' expense by slashing Basic Customs Duty on several items which
are shared with States, and shifting the difference to the Agricultural Infrastructure
and Development Cess (AIDC), which the Centre keeps entirely. This ensures
that States lose out. So, the AIDC's share is increased, the Centre deprives the
States of their rightful revenue. Its growing addiction to Cesses is not only
opaque, but also undermines fiscal federalism. Yet for all its love of cesses, there
is one the Centre just cannot seem to stomach, the GST Compensation Cess,
which was intended to offset the loss of revenue to States after the GST
implementation, and has been extended only until March 2026. That money
actually belongs to the States. The States have repeatedly demanded that their
share in vertical devolution be raised from 41 per cent of revenues to 50 per cent,
a reasonable demand, but one that continues to be ignored.
Once the Central Finance Commission submits its report, it dissolves,
leaving the States with no Constitutional mechanism to challenge or negotiate
their share. Where is the accountability? We could have an independent fiscal
council to serve as a redressal body, ensuring that the States have a say in how
their hard-earned revenue is allocated. I urge the Finance Minister to consider
such a method. This is because should not those who contribute the most to the
nation’s economy have a voice to receive a fairer share? Or can the cycle of
economic injustice just continue unchecked?
Let me turn to my own State because Kerala’s share of tax out of the
divisible pool seems to be on a permanent downward slide, from 3.8 per cent
24.03.2025 964
under the 10th Finance Commission (1980-85) to three per cent in 2000-05. Now,
under the 15th Finance Commission, it is down to a mere 1.9 per cent. Rs. 18,000
crore have been lost. This is for a State which is struggling to meet routine
expenditures. It is sinking into debt that has now crossed Rs.4 lakh crore. But as
always, the expectation of Kerala seems to be, pay more, receive less, and
somehow keep thriving.
Amid this financial crunch, when the State Government sought Rs.4,500
crore in special assistance to tackle coastal erosion, rehabilitate fisherfolk through
the Punargeham project, and modernize fishing harbours, it was given no
response. Lakhs of poor fishermen are left struggling under a trifecta of crises –
low prices for their catch, depleted fish stocks due to overfishing, and the removal
of kerosene subsidies by this Government for their boat engines. Yet, no relief
package has been announced. Even Kerala’s Rs.1,000 crore request to address
human-wildlife conflicts was ignored. This is part of a larger pattern of neglect
evident in the plight of the people who are victims of wildlife attacks, fishermen
left adrift, ASHA workers still deemed volunteers despite their critical role, and
key projects left unfunded.
While the Centre has earmarked Rs.25 lakh crore for the States, Kerala
receives less than Rs.40,000 crore, which is a drop in the ocean. The State also
failed to secure a Rs.24,000 crore economic package for financial stabilization
and the much-needed aid for the Vizhinjam port project in the current Budget. All
we have got, and that too after the intervention of the hon. Governor, is a loan of
Rs. 5,900 crore and no grant. Apparently, when it comes to equitable financial
24.03.2025 965
support, some States are more equal than others.
The Centre’s masterclass in delay tactics continues. Wayanad’s landslide
victims are still waiting for aid that never seems to arrive, despite the Kerala High
Court actually urging the Government for immediate action. The State
Government conducted a Post-Disaster Needs Assessment and estimated that
Rs.2,220 crore is required for recovery and reconstruction. This was submitted to
the Centre on November 13, 2024; nothing has come. And this is supposed to be
an urgent disaster relief.
Instead of releasing much-needed funds like this, the Centre still insists that
Kerala must first exhaust its own balance in the State Disaster Response Fund
for all other disasters before receiving any help for Wayanad. All this is there,
while thousands of lives and livelihoods are at stake. But it is part of a larger
pattern of neglect and unfulfilled promises. From healthcare and infrastructure to
livelihood support, Kerala has been systematically sidelined. I have been pleading
in this House many times for help in addressing the severe coastal erosion
problem. My own constituency has lost 64 sq. km. of territory to the sea while I
have been MP. Bharat Mata ki Dharti, is lost forever. But the Centre says it is not
their problem, and the State says they have no money for the sea walls and
groynes needed to save our coasts. Beaches where youth played football or
cricket in my first term as an MP have disappeared today. Who is going to help
us?
Key projects that could transform Kerala’s infrastructure and economy
continue to be stuck in limbo. The All-India Institute of Medical Sciences remains
24.03.2025 966
a mirage despite many years of repeated requests. Madam, 22 AIIMS have been
sanctioned across India; Kerala is still waiting. Similarly, the National Institute of
Speech and Hearing in Thiruvananthapuram was promised an upgrade to a
National University for Rehabilitation and Disability Studies in the Union Budget of
2015-16. This is the explicit commitment. There is no action 10 years later. A
promise to establish a National University for Ayurveda in Thiruvananthapuram
has recently materialised in Gujarat.
It seems that where Kerala is concerned, promises are easier to make than
to keep.
Now, while these delays and denials are Kerala’s burden, other aspects of
the Indian economic landscape are very worrying for other States, for all of our
country. The Economic Survey suggests that foreign investment is booming. But,
in fact, the net FDI has been slowing year after year and outflows are surging. In
just April to October last year, a whopping 34.1 billion dollars left the country
through disinvestment and repatriation, which is an increase from 26 billion
dollars the previous year. India’s share of global FDI, even excluding China, is
shrinking. But sure, let us keep celebrating token investment announcements
while the real money quietly walks out of the door.
It is not just a foreign investment that is pulling back, the Indian private
investment has also been shrinking falling from 25 per cent of GDP in Dr.
Manmohan Singh’s time in 2008 to just 13-14 per cent of GDP since 2020. What
is the culprit? It is weak domestic demand, that has dampened confidence and
kept businesses from putting their money back into the economy. And we still
24.03.2025 967
expect to believe that everything is nice on track for Viksit Bharat.
Where are the decisive measures to drive export-led growth, to seize the
“China Plus One” opportunity, and to revive investments and manufacturing?
Does India not wish to absorb the capital leaving China? What are we doing
about it? We are abandoning PLI, and this Bill offers nothing new to inspire
economic confidence. The service sector alone cannot drive broad-based growth,
industrial expansion is essential to create jobs and build economic resilience, and
we must say that there is no large national strategy in this Bill to achieve that.
To conclude, Madam Chair, we are left with a Finance Bill, devoid of vision
and lacking in resolve. The people of India deserved decisive action and forward-
thinking policies, yet their aspirations have been set aside. If Viksit Bharat is to
come, it will be built by those who are currently unemployed, who have no money
to spend, and few opportunities to improve their lives.
I urge the Finance Minister and the Government to think about them. India
requires and deserves a strategy that addresses their deepest needs and
aspirations. And sadly, we are yet to see one in this Bill.
Thank you, Madam Chairperson. Jai Hind.

24.03.2025 988
SUSHRI MAHUA MOITRA (KRISHNANAGAR): Hon. Chairperson Madam, I
have been allotted 18 minutes by my Party. So, I hope that I will be able to get
the entire 18 minutes.
I rise today on behalf of the All India Trinamool Congress to dissect and
oppose the Finance Bill, 2025. Albert Einstein famously said that the hardest
thing in the world is to understand income tax. Similarly, we find it very hard to
understand why this Government’s taxation policy continues to widen the stark
divide between the two Indias – a Kuber’s India for the elite and the privileged,
and another Vishwakarma’s India for the masses who are bearing the brunt of
this Government’s economic mismanagement.
We have just finished the spectacle of the Maha Kumbh with faith drawing
millions to the holy Sangam at Prayag. The Government is patting itself on its
back for organising the grandest spectacle that humankind has ever seen. It is a
small concern that the same UP Government, which was unable to count the
number of deaths in the Kumbh stampedes and gave the country a number, was
magically able to insist that precisely 63 crore people had bathed in the river.
Now, 63 crore is the entire adult population of India, which statistically implies that
every single one of us sitting here has bathed in the Kumbh. I certainly have not
bathed in the Kumbh. Most of us sitting around me have not bathed in the
Kumbh. But this mirage continues. This great mirage is continuing, similarly,
much like the great taxation mirage that this Government has created.
In December 2024, in response to a Parliamentary Question, the Finance
Ministry provided certain crucial statistics on taxpayers in this country. What did
24.03.2025 989
we learn? We learned that there are eight crore people who file tax returns in this
country. But out of that, only 56 lakh people earn more than Rs.15 lakh per
annum. These 56 lakh individuals are the engine of India’s entrepreneurial and
services economy. They are the only ones who are effectively paying their
income tax. Now, with a new exemption structure, those earning up to Rs.12.5
lakh annually will pay zero tax. Let us be clear. Income tax is leviable only on 56
lakh people out of 140 crore population. Yet, we have a massive Income Tax
Department with supernormal, snooping, police-like powers and unchecked
discretion, operating under the pretence of enforcement. What is the purpose of
this? The 56 lakh people at least have a graded tax system. But for the rest of
India, Vishwakarma’s India, there is no relief. For the 139 crore people in
Vishwakarma’s India, GST is the great equaliser, but in the most regressive way
possible. In financial year 2023-24, the Government of India collected Rs.20 lakh
crore in GST, which comes to about Rs.15,000 per person. So, a billionaire and
a daily wage earner, both pay GST on food, on transport and on essential
commodities. The Government has done nothing to alleviate this burden.
There is no discussion on reducing indirect taxes on essential commodities.
There is no roadmap to ensure fairer wealth distribution.
All social sector spending has actually seen contractions. Let me give you a
few examples. The Finance Bill mentions a health and education cess of four per
cent on income tax. There is no problem. Everyone is happy to pay this if it goes
in the right way. You are taking our money. It is fine. The Kothari Commission, in
1966, had said that six per cent of India’s GDP must be spent on education. The
24.03.2025 990
successive National Educational Policies including NEP-2020 insisted that six per
cent of GDP must be spent on education. What are we spending today? We are
spending around 4.5 per cent. What is China spending? China is spending 6.2
per cent.
This Government’s own National Health Policy of 2017 said that public
health must account for 2.5 per cent of GDP. What is this Government spending
today? In the last Budget, it spent 1.94 per cent. We must be the only country
with a declining spending in health. In the last few years, we have come down
from two per cent to 1.94 per cent. So, Madam Finance Minister, wherever you
are, I hope you are listening to me. Tax-payers do not mind paying extra cess for
health and education. But, at least, spend it. Give the country what it deserves.
Food subsidies have been slashed even as inflation has bitten into rural
households. Between January and December of 2024, rural inflation was 5.4 per
cent, a full hundred basis points higher than urban inflation, mainly driven by
higher food prices.
MGNREGA is still under-funded. Last year’s allocation was Rs. 86,000
crore. This year’s allocation is also Rs. 86,000 crore, even though there is a
higher demand. If you pay West Bengal its Rs. 7000 crore, which is due, then,
you decrease the outlay even more.
This time, you have guillotined the Demands for Grants concerning the
Department of Food and Public Distribution. There was no discussion. Yet, there
are certain points that everyone needs to know. This country has a National Food
Security Act since 2013, which mandates that 75 per cent of rural households and
24.03.2025 991
50 per cent of urban households must have coverage. Today, 81 crore people are
getting 5 kilograms of free rations per month by this Government. This is
dependent on the 2011 Census. Now, the Government, on affidavit, told the
Supreme Court that we have not had a Census in 2011 and we do not even know
when the next Census is going to be. So, the Government says, well, how are
you going to revise the numbers? There are many poor people who are not
getting rations. The new number is: an extra 13 crore must be given rations. The
Government says, ‘We do not know. We do not have a Census’. So, the Supreme
Court said, ‘What do you have’? We have an unorganised migrant portal, the e-
SHRAM Portal. According to the e-SHRAM portal, eight crore new people must
be given rations. The Government is steadfastly refusing to give this eight-crore
people rations. Please note that these people are paying GST. They are paying
GST. You are raising Rs. 20 lakh crore based on these people, yet you are not
giving these eight crore people rations per month according to the Food Security
Act. Now, I am not comfortable with this kind of an inequity. We certainly do not
want this on our conscience. Certainly not.
You keep saying ‘Democracy’, ‘Democracy’, ‘Democracy’. The hon. Prime
Minister, in a podcast, all over the world, was talking about freedom to criticise
and democracy and democratic processes. We welcome it. But for a democracy
to function, it is vital that its electoral processes be above board and above
question.
The Legislative Department in the Law Ministry is the nodal Department
which controls all spending for elections, for polls, and for appointment of the poll
24.03.2025 992
members. The hon. Finance Minister, this year, allocated Rs. 300 crore for voter
ID cards; allocated Rs. 600 crore for election expenses; and allocated Rs. 19
crore for EVMs. But here, we have an Election Commission that is refusing to
give 100 per cent VVPAT paper trails. It is refusing to make 17C forms public. It is
refusing to give us new additions and new deletions to the voter list. It is refusing
to explain how duplicate EPIC cards are being issued in Murshidabad and
Gujarat. So, spend tax-payers’ money but use it in a right way. We need some
answers.
Now, let us examine the myth and reality of this Government’s growth
story. The hon. Finance Minister claimed that India will grow at 6.5 per cent next
year. This forecast, by the way, is downward from 9.2 per cent predicted in 2023-
24. Just before he became the RBI Governor, former Revenue Secretary, Sanjay
Malhotra, in December, warned Government’s tax officials that they should not kill
the proverbial golden goose with their demands.
Net foreign inflows fell to Rs. 1.2 billion between April and December, down
from Rs. 7.8 billion. Private investment is weak, manufacturing is stagnating at 17
per cent of GDP and rural unemployment is nine per cent. Fiscal deficit has come
down to 4.4 percent, agreed, but that has not happened because of revenue
growth. That has happened because you have cut essential spending to the poor.
This is economic mismanagement, not fiscal prudence.
The Government’s obsession with corporate incentives has only favoured
big businessmen where small businesses and labourers have got hurt. You
slashed corporate income tax in 2019 from 30 per cent to 22 per cent. Has private
24.03.2025 993
investment picked up? No. So, who have these tax cuts benefited? Let me look at
just one example. Let us not forget that all tax rules are written for

it comes to crony capitalists, there are special rules. Please listen. In 2016, there
was a rule that there can be no SEZ which has only a power plant; no power
plant, only SEZ. But for a very special friend, Mr. A, this was done in Godda,
Jharkhand. Mr. A received not only billions in benefits during capex phase but
receives an ongoing benefit of no import duty for importing coal, no coal cess, no
domestic supply obligation. Savings of clean energy cess were Rs. 320 crore per
annum. Customs tax and GST benefits were another Rs. 2000 crore. Now, the
things with Bangladesh have soured. The Government has said you can sell
power domestically but have you recovered the back interest and the taxes which
were meant for the SEZs? The plant was built by a Chinese contractor with
Chinese equipment, customs duty free, GST free, tax free, not using Jharkhand
coal, not giving Jharkhand power, not paying clean energy cess and currently,
employing less than 1000 people. For some people, rules, laws and Acts are
nothing. ‘गरीब लहर(cid:332) पे पहर े िबठाए जाते ह(cid:167), सम(cid:198)दर(cid:332) क(cid:236) तलाशी कोई नह(cid:233) लेता ।’

Hon. Chairperson, the Government’s draconian taxation policies have
turned our stock market into a breeding ground for tax uncertainty. Let us
24.03.2025 995
examine the facts. They are ming-boggling. In 2004, capital gains tax was

this long term capital gains was hiked to 12.5 per cent. Short term capital gains
was increased from 10 per cent to 15 per cent in 2018 to 20 per cent in 2025.
Securities transaction tax has been hiked by 60 per cent in 2024. Corporate
dividends, once taxed at 15 per cent, are now taxed at slab rates with TDS. The
Government and SEBI have together destroyed wealth creation in the stock
market, both domestic and foreign. Europe, the US, Singapore, etc., are
aggressively attracting investors, but we are driving them away with policy
arrogance and excessive taxation.

SHRI LAVU SRIKRISHNA DEVARAYALU (NARASARAOPET): Thank you for
allowing me to speak on the Finance Bill, 2025. I have four areas of focus.
Before I get into that, let me start off by congratulating the hon. Finance
Minister for two issues. The first issue is that the indirect tax collection has risen
by almost 16.2 per cent compared to the last year. The second issue for which I
want to congratulate her is with regard to tax exemption which is up to Rs.12 lakh
given to the income tax payers. There is almost Rs.1 lakh crore that has been
foregone by the Government. But still, the assumption is that by next year, there
will be an increase of 13.4 per cent in tax receipts. This shows the confidence in
the Indian economy and in the Indian financial system. I congratulate her for
these two issues.
Now, I would come to the four focus areas that I referred to. The first one is
regarding the faceless e-assessment. I have data from 2017-18 to 2021-22. This
faceless assessment has made the system very transparent and efficient. There
are almost 5,98,448 cases that have been filed during this period of 2017 to 2021.
Out of these close to 6 lakh appeals, about 73 per cent of the appeals have been
disposed of. But still there are 27 per cent of the appeals that have not been
disposed of. So, at any point of time, the amount that is being locked up as these
appeals have not been disposed of, is almost Rs.58 lakh crore. So, if I extrapolate
that of the disposed of cases, that is, 73 per cent and calculate based on the 27
per cent cases which have not been disposed of, it is almost Rs.15 lakh crore of
money that has been locked up in the system. I would request the hon. Finance
Minister to clarify on the way forward so that this locked up amount can be
24.03.2025 1007
released.
The second focus area is with regard to the GST collection. It has grown by
almost 77 per cent from Rs.11 lakh crore in 2020 to Rs.20 lakh crore in 2023-24.
16.00 hrs
However, with the growth in the GST collections, there are also pending
appeals that have grown from 5,000 to 15,000 in the last five years. This is almost
three times the growth. So, the way forward consists of the following
approaches:- The first is, whether we are going to take the 15th Finance
Commission’s recommendation and make certain changes to the rates, merging
them to simplify the GST, making it easier for the GST payers. The second way
forward is that there are many Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunals at the
State level that are not functional. I think the Union Government has to handhold
the States and make these tribunals functional. If we implement these two
measures, the pending appeals that have been increasing will definitely be
curtailed, which will, in turn, add to the revenues of the State.
The third focus area is the custom duty collection. In the 2025-26 Budget
presented by the Finance Minister, the expectation from custom duties is around
Rs. 2,40,000 crore. This accounts for almost 5.6 per cent of the gross tax
revenues. However, we have been hearing for the last three or four months since
Trump has come into power in the US that there will be changes in the tariff
regime that will take effect in the coming months. Therefore, it is uncertain
whether this Rs. 2,40,000 crore can be maintained with the new tariff regime. If it
is reduced, how can this be compensated, and from where can it be
24.03.2025 1008
compensated? If the Finance Minister can clarify this, it would be greatly
appreciated.
The fourth focus point is regarding the total CAPEX. The total CAPEX
spent in the last 10 years is Rs. 50,00,000 crore, which is an amazing
achievement. With this investment, our railways have been ranked among the top
five globally; our metros have become the third largest in the world; our airports
have become the third largest domestic aviation market in the world; and our
national highways have become the second largest in the world. This has been
achieved within 10 years. Achieving this is not easy, but it has been possible due
to the significant investments made in infrastructure over the last decade.
However, in the last two years, particularly, there has been an under-
utilization of the CAPEX. Last year, there was an under-utilization from Rs.
74,000 crore to Rs. 75,000 crore, and the year before that, it was almost Rs.
25,000 crore. In total, there has been Rs. 1,00,000 crore of under-utilization of
CAPEX in the last two years. Why I am mentioning about this underutilization is
because of this reason. Instead of keeping it under-utilized, can we bring it into
the system? How can we do that? Can we invest in the new technologies that are
emerging? We can invest in R&D activities. Why do we have to invest in the R&D
activities and in the new technologies? It is because if we look at where the
foreign institutional investors in the last three or four years have invested across
the globe, they are mostly interested in investing in new technologies rather than
hard infrastructure projects. If we can redirect this under-utilized CAPEX into
these new technologies, foreign investors will also be encouraged to invest, which
24.03.2025 1009
will increase tax revenues. I hope the Finance Minister will take this into account.
Additionally, I cannot discuss the Finance Bill without mentioning Andhra
Pradesh. Everyone in the House knows how Andhra Pradesh was bifurcated, and
it was done very unscientifically. We had a revenue deficit of Rs. 16,000 crore in
2014-15 when the State was bifurcated. The most shocking aspect is that when
the national share of the agriculture sector has reduced from 17 per cent to 14.4
per cent in the last 10 years, from 2014 to 2024, do you know what has happened
in Andhra Pradesh? In Andhra Pradesh, our reliance on the agriculture sector has
increased from 24 per cent in 2014 to 35 per cent in 2024. We have reverted to a
primary economic model. Even in the service sector, which is a tertiary economic
activity, our share was 51 per cent in 2014, but it has decreased to 41 per cent.
Our entire economy has been reversed, and we have backslidden into an
agriculture-dependent economy. In contrast, States like Telangana, Karnataka,
and other States have capitalized on the service sector that has been prevalent in
their regions. In the last 10 years, we have become revenue deficient while
simultaneously becoming increasingly reliant on agricultural activities.
So, the request from our side is to understand the issues of Andhra
Pradesh and give us the revenue deficit grant that we have been asking for the
State of Andhra Pradesh.
It is not only because of bifurcation of the State in an unscientific way, but
we also have been facing troubles because of the financial mismanagement that
has happened in Andhra Pradesh in the last five years. You all know, when we
talk about Andhra Pradesh, the first thing that comes to anyone’s mind in this
24.03.2025 1010
House and everywhere is about the big-budgeted movies of Andhra Pradesh.
People talk about Baahubali, RRR, and Pushpa. They all collected Rs. 1,700
crore, Rs. 1,800 crore, and Rs. 2,000 crore. They all have collected huge
amounts. But I want to put across a story in this House which has much more
value and much more collections that have happened within the last five years in
the State of Andhra Pradesh.
You must have heard about Delhi liquor scam, and whatever you have
heard about Delhi liquor scam and whatever you have heard about the numbers
in the Delhi liquor scam, when you compare it with what has happened in Andhra
Pradesh, it is just peanuts.
16.06 hrs (Shri Dilip Saikia in the Chair)
The repercussions are seen in the Parliament. There is a Rajya Sabha MP
who resigned from his post before his term has ended even though he has four
years of term left. Even then, he has resigned and said: “I am quitting politics
because of liquor scam that has happened in Andhra Pradesh”.
In this liquor scam also, just like in the movies like RRR, Pushpa, there is
pre-production, production and distribution. Even for the liquor scam there was a
pre-production, production and distribution that had happened. What is a pre-
production? Pre-production was that the narrative was built. … (Interruptions)
SHRI MADDILA GURUMOORTHY (TIRUPATI): Sir, he is misleading the House.
… (Interruptions)
SHRI LAVU SRIKRISHNA DEVARAYALU: The narrative was built at the pre-
production level. … (Interruptions) There, it was mentioned that once they come
24.03.2025 1011
into power, they will definitely prohibit alcohol in the State. … (Interruptions) This
narrative was built. … (Interruptions) If they did not prohibit alcohol, the rates of
alcohol will be raised to such a level that normal people cannot consume it. This
was the narrative that was built at the pre-production level. … (Interruptions)
What happened after that? After that, once the production stage has come,
they have gone to the companies which are producing alcohol and said: “We are
raising the prices. Do you want to give it or not?” They were all international
brands and they said: “We cannot supply alcohol at this rate”. So, they quit. What
happened at the production level was that all the distilleries in the State, that is
around 25 distilleries, were taken over. It is not only that those distilleries were
taken over, but on top of that what had happened was that 26 new companies
were incorporated. Adan was incorporated in December, 2019; Graysons; Leela;
JR Associates; PV Spirits were all incorporated in March, 2020. So, almost 26
new companies were incorporated for production of alcohol.
What had happened was that once those companies were incorporated,
they started producing new brands which no one in this country ever heard of. I
will read out the name of these brands. SNJ 1000, British Empire, President’s
Medal -- even President was not left out -- HD Gold Whiskey, Anytime Gold, etc.
All these brands were brought into the market, which were never heard of and
never seen. They all were brought into the market by these distilleries which have
come up within a few days after 2019.
What these brands have done at the production level? How will they be
distributed once these brands are produced? They have given these brands to
24.03.2025 1012
AP State Breweries Corporation Limited (APSBCL) and once they have given it to
the APSBCL, they were sold in the shops owned by the Government.
How did they sell it in the shops? It is a very interesting question. They
have given the beverage to all the shops and once they are given, all the
transactions were cash transactions. There is no cashless transaction. …
(Interruptions)
Sir, I will conclude in one minute. I have 15-minute time. I will conclude. …
(Interruptions) There was no digital payment for it. I will give you the numbers.
Out of Rs. 99,000 crore worth products that were sold in the retail shops in those
five years between 2019 and 2024, only transactions worth Rs. 650 crore were
cashless transactions. … (Interruptions) So, Rs. 99,000 crore worth transactions
were in cash that has happened whereas in the country it was reverse. The UPI
transactions had gone up from Rs. 1,00,000 crore in 2017-2018 to Rs. 200 lakh
crore in five years, that is 138 per cent increase whereas in Andhra Pradesh, on
the sale of liquor, it has been reverse. … (Interruptions)
I will complete in one minute. I will mention the last point.
How has this been looted out? Due to this, there is mismanagement of Rs.
18,000 crore. Whose money is this? This money belongs to the SC, ST, OBC and
minority communities. It belongs to every citizen of this country. Not only Rs.
18,000 crore, there is Rs. 4,000 crore which has also been taken out of Andhra
Pradesh through benami transactions and parked in Dubai and Africa. This has
been done by Shri N. Sunil Reddy. He used the face of an infrastructure company
in Hyderabad to park Rs. 2000 crore in Dubai. Another Rs. 1,000 crore has been
24.03.2025 1013
parked in Africa. All this money belongs to Andhra Pradesh. … (Interruptions) We
request the ED to investigate the issue. These are the reports. The SIT can only
investigate to a certain extent but the ED is the one which can verify the
transactions that are made fraudulently.
Further, I request that the revenue deficit grant be given and the
Government should deliberate on bringing liquor under GST.
