SHRI ANIL YESHWANT DESAI (MUMBAI SOUTH-CENTRAL): Sir, the Finance
Bill, 2025 is being discussed and debated in this august House. I would like to
mention a few important aspects of the Bill, and of course, would be seeking
clarifications to the same when the hon. Finance Minister replies to the House.
Describing the vision of Viksit Bharat, that is developed India, the hon.
Finance Minister identifies agriculture, MSMEs, investment and exports at the
macro-level as its four powerful engines, with reform as its fuel and inclusivity as
its guiding spirit. The budget focuses on various development measures,
including boosting manufacturing, furthering `Make in India’, enabling
employment-led development, investing in people, the economy and innovation,
securing and enhancing energy supplies, promoting exports and curbing imports
to achieve the goals of Atmanirbahar Bharat.
24.03.2025 1017
The hon. Finance Minister announced a slew of measures to spur the
agricultural growth and productivity, thereby benefiting the Annadaata. A sum of
Rs. 1.37 lakh crore has been allocated to the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers
Welfare, which is 2.5 per cent lower than the Revised Estimates of 2024-25.
Further, measures have been introduced to be implemented to make
agriculture a lucrative occupation, and therefore, steps are proposed like easy
access to affordable credit, adequate availability of fertilizers, access to quality
seeds, remunerative pricing and agriculture produce, thereby doubling the income
of farmers. However, the schemes like Dhan Dhanya Krishi Yojana, which
focuses on 100 underperforming agricultural districts, do not provide structural
reforms in supply chains, MSP guarantees or crop diversification. There is no
clear roadmap for tackling the farmer’s distress, climate change impact on
agriculture or rising costs. The reliance on NAFED or NCCF for procurement of
pulses may not be sufficient to ensure stable prices.
Over the years, agricultural crop insurance has not been of significant help
to the farmers in reality. In reality, on the ground, they are kept away from getting
bank credit by way of farm loans on the pretext of low CIBIL score. This results in
continued unabated suicides in Marathwada and Vidarbha regions of
Maharashtra. This is a worrying cause and the Government should ensure that
Anna Dada gets his rightful dues.
Incidentally, Maharashtra, including Mumbai, the financial capital of the
country, contributes the most to the Central exchequer, that is, around 40 per
cent of the taxes. What it gets back? It gets back meagrely six per cent from the
24.03.2025 1018
devolution of Central funds. I think that should be also revisited while working it
out, and whatever recommendations come from the Finance Commission need to
be reviewed.
Sir, the support to the MSMEs and start-ups lacks the depth they require.
The Government launched a fund of funds for start-ups in June 2016 with the
corpus of Rs. 10,000 crore. Under this Scheme, the Government provides capital
to alternative investment funds. They in turn, invest money in startups through
equity-linked instruments. A new fund named ‘Deep Tech Fund of Funds' has
been proposed to encourage next-generation startups from technical and non-
technical backgrounds. Also credit enhancements to MSMEs have been
proposed in this Budget. However, it does not significantly improve access to low
interest credit for small businesses. Credit card limit of Rs. 5 lakh for micro-
enterprises may be insufficient for expansion. Moreover, the lack of clarity on
implementation and fund disbursement may slowdown the business growth.
As far as direct employment is concerned, the Government should focus on
the potential in public sector, Central Government Undertakings like Railways for
employment. The public sector banks, insurance companies, oil companies and
railways have tremendous potential to see recruitment at the entry level for the
youth. However, these public sector units are employing youth on one-year
apprenticeship basis. That is happening in the banks and in the insurance
companies too. On completion of one year, the youth, who comes in as an
apprentice, is left high and dry. This leads to frustration in the young generation.
This not only renders them jobless but it affects their family environment too.
24.03.2025 1019
The Government should do away with the one-year apprenticeship jobs
and offer permanent employment to the youth of our nation. Instead of
strengthening the public sector, the Government takes more interest in
disinvestment and selling away the public sector companies to a selected few
private players. The general insurance companies is the latest example. The
employees of the general insurance companies are still awaiting their wage
revision. Whatever conditions were put by the Government, they have also been
met by the companies. As far as their solvency ratio is concerned, which is very
vital as far as insurance companies’ profitability and running of their business are
concerned, they have achieved that well beyond that.
As far as infrastructure and investment is concerned Rs. 11.21 lakh crore
for infrastructure have been allocated with an aim to encourage higher private
participation. The infrastructure-related Ministries are to draw out a plan of
pipeline of projects for three years in PPP mode. The States are also
encouraged for the same. However, PPP mode has had mixed success till now
due to delays, funding issues and disputes. Rs. 1.5 lakh crore interest-free loans
to the States may lead to misallocation of funds without strict accountability.
An Export Promotion Mission, with sectoral and ministerial targets driven
jointly by the Ministries of Commerce, MSME and Finance, is to be set up and
Bharat Trade Net for international trade is to be set up as a unified platform for
trade documentation and financing solutions. Similarly, a national framework is to
be formulated as guidance to the States for promoting global capacity centres in
emerging two-tier cities. These measures aim to boost exports, but they do not
24.03.2025 1020
address trade imbalances, raw material shortages, or global competition. Our
trade policies still face high tariffs and regulatory bottlenecks, which may make it
difficult to compete in the global markets despite export incentives. Moreover, the
US President’s announcement of reciprocal tariffs, which is to come on 2nd April,
may further affect the bilateral trade ties. I hope that the Government is well
prepared on this to deal with the United States. I hope that it will score brownie
points for the nation.
Sir nuclear energy investment raises safety and cost concerns. Rs. 20,000
crore outlay for small modular reactors raises concerns about safety, nuclear
waste disposal mechanism and regulatory preparedness, especially regarding
agreement on civil liability for nuclear damage as amendments to nuclear liability
laws may also increase risk for public safety and shifting of responsibility away
from the private operators.
An allocation of Rs. 1 lakh crore to the healthcare sector in this budget
does look a healthy provision. While day-care cancer centres and PM Jan Arogya
Yojana for gig workers are introduced, the overall healthcare budget lacks
substantial expansion. There is no comprehensive policy to improve rural
healthcare access. Shortages of doctors and paramedical staff, and deficiency of
medicines in the Government hospitals are the glaring examples of the plight of
our poor public health infrastructure.
The budget shows allocation of Rs.1.28 lakh crore for education with an
increase of 6.22 per cent over the last year’s provision. However, the provision for
education in the budget is 4.6 per cent of the GDP. It remains below NEP targets
24.03.2025 1021
and lags global peers. The ever-widening gap between urban and rural education
needs to be bridged if the purpose of education to all, and the maximum literacy
percentage is to be achieved. Heavy investment in Al, digital education and
geospatial infrastructure may further widen the urban-rural divide as
underprivileged population will be deprived of the same. What are the
arrangements on equal distribution of resources that have been made by the
Government in this regard?
In this budget, the hon. Finance Minister has exempted the salaried middle-
class up to earnings of Rs. 12 lakh per annum. This has, no doubt, given much
needed relief to the middle-class. But at the same time, the Government has
given nod to the 8th Pay Commission which may become operational in the year
2026. Therefore, the relief given in this budget may be washed out. At the same
time, indirect taxes in the form of GST with several slab bands leaves the middle-
class with hardly anything in their hands, which otherwise, they would have saved
either in banks or spent in the market for their increasing needs. The GST rates
need to be rationalised and also the GST of 18 per cent levied on insurance
policies needs to brought down or curtailed, which is demanded by crores of
policyholders of the nation.
The fiscal deficit is estimated at 4.4 per cent of the GDP, which is although
lower than the previous years, remains a significant concern for fiscal
sustainability. The heavy reliance on gross market borrowings, which have gone
to Rs. 14.82 lakh crore, increases India’s debt burden, raising concerns about
repayment capacity of our country and potential credit rating downgrades.
24.03.2025 1022
The Budget focuses on agriculture, MSMEs, investment, and exports, as
engines of growth. However, fiscal deficit, uncertain execution of schemes and
over reliance on private investments, pose major challenges. While tax benefits,
infrastructure, and digital expansions are positive, the lack of direct employment
support, healthcare expansion, and structural economic reforms may slowdown
inclusive growth.
And last but not least, the hon. Finance Minister may enlighten this House
about the PLI scheme, which was introduced in the year 2020, in 14 key sectors,
with the objective of boosting manufacturing, and thus, addressing the very
important issue of unemployment in our country. The manufacturing sector’s
contribution to the GDP is only 15 per cent, which needs to go in another couple
of years to 25 per cent, a target which has been set. The hon. Finance Minister
may, in her reply, answer to this.
Thank you very much for giving me this chance to express my views on this
Finance Bill. Thank you.
SHRIMATI SUPRIYA SULE (BARAMATI): Sir, I stand here today to speak on
the Finance Bill.
What is really the purpose of the Finance Bill? The purpose is to give effect
to the financial proposals of the Central Government for the financial year 2025-
26. What we heard is a rhetoric from the Treasury Benches. It was digging up the
coffins of the bygone era. It was unfortunate because I was really optimistic to
hear something more from the Treasure Benches. But it was just the old era of
the last five decades of India’s history.
24.03.2025 1023
India is really going through a challenging time because the globe is going
through a challenging time. There are disruptions or changes in various
dispensations all over the world, especially what is happening in America which
we are all reading. Right or wrong, only the time will judge it and the history will
judge it. But as of now, where we are standing is the headwinds of the geo-
political situation; is the word ‘uncertainty’ right now. And what is happening in
India versus the uncertainty, is what really this Finance Bill stands for.
I will quickly mark out a few points which really, I think, we need to know
and the Finance Ministry needs to take cognisance of. I am not standing here as
just an opponent or a critic. But I am concerned as a citizen also, who is
representing 23 lakh stakeholders today and who are directly impacted by the
decisions that we take here. And that number is only multiplied by 500 MPs here
and 200 MPs there; about 800 MPs.
So, growth is at 5.4 per cent today, where manufacturing is sluggish, and
food inflation is disrupted. There are good days and there are bad days. I would
not say that it is higher all the time because there are some good days. So, I am
putting facts on the table. Jobs are clearly stagnant. There is a gap in rising trade.
Capital flows are disappointing. Urban consumption is weak, which I will come
back to. Then, the private consumption is down. Exports have shrunk by 10.9 per
cent. I am not only blaming the Government. I understand it. I may not be an
economist but I do understand that we have to take ownership of it. I am not just
criticising but the geopolitical situation, which I go back to, is one of the big
reasons behind the same. It does not mean that Indian products are bad. But the
24.03.2025 1024
situation is such that people are sceptical saying, ‘Oh My God, I will ship today,
and if something else happens in the next few weeks to come -- as we have
expected something to happen between 2nd and 4th April -- then what happens to
my goods’. So, that is the concern, urgency, and the panic in the market right
now. Exports have shrunk.
The prices of manufactured products have increased by 2.86 per cent right
now compared to what it was in January, which was 2.51 per cent.
Unemployment is at 4.2 per cent. There are 15 per cent youth today who are
jobless, and the number grows every day.
Now, I come to car sales. I come from Pune district, Maharashtra. Pune
district is one of the largest hubs of car manufacturing. We have Tata. We have
Bajaj. All the big hubs are there. We have Mercedes Benz. All have invested
there. The car and motorcycle markets have gone down by 17 per cent. Sir, I can
authenticate this if anybody wants. There are three reasons all of them are
saying. One is affordability. It is a challenge today, that is high interest rates to
buy it and is slowing down of the economy. So, these are all the indicators which
are showing that the economy is in trouble. So rather than riding and saying
everything is wonderful, I think with full humility, let us put all our minds here
together to find how we are going to ride this tiger which is waiting for us in the
next few months to come or weeks to come because of the global situation.
Sir, the PLI scheme which my colleague talked about, what are really the
outcomes of these schemes because these are all interventions which came from
the treasury, these are their interventions but I really would like to ask them. Now,
24.03.2025 1025
this is where the economy is.
Now, we come down to breaking up of the taxation. Now, there is the
Finance Commission which has proposed reduction of federal tax revenues. That
is what we are reading in the newspapers. What has been suggested by the
Government is that normally, the tax is divided and we were earlier at various
percentages. Now, we reached 40 per cent. Then, there was 41 per cent agreed
to be given to the States. Now, they are saying to roll back and make it 40 per
cent retrospectively. First of all, we have lost so much with GST. I come from the
State of Maharashtra. We have the State of Tamil Nadu. We are all States which
give and contribute to the kitty and we are happy to do that. If we can help other
States grow, we are very proud, we will work harder and make sure we can give
more to the kitty but it cannot be at just the cost of our tax. So, I would like to ask
that if from 41 per cent we come to 40 per cent, the difference is going to be of
Rs. 35,000 crore which the Central Government is going to get. So, that is our
money plus the cesses and the surcharges. This is surreptitiously done. I would
like to put it on record there are monies which are supposed to be shared by the
States but these are taken in the form of cesses by the Central Government and
the States suffer because of that and all our States are suffering for that. So, what
is this mechanism? It is completely unfair. It is not fair on the States which are
already suffering. We all know that all the FIIs have pulled out of the stock
market. Why are people pulling out of the Indian economy? They are pulling out
because they do not see the growth happening here. They prefer other
economies which are growing. So, what steps is this Government to take? Who
24.03.2025 1026
is affected in this entire stock market? It is the middle class which is getting hurt.
Right now, these foreigners have pulled out to such a large extent, it is the Indian
economy, the producers and the Indian middleclass which has held on to it. There
has been a correction of over 10 per cent today in the stock market. So, what
checks and balances is this Government going to do to make sure that they stay
stead?
Another very big intervention which we need to flag here is the gold loan.
The gold loan is something about which even the RBI has shown concern. This is
the first time it has happened in India that over 50 per cent people are turning
towards the gold loan. What is it signalling? The RBI actually has red flagged it.
And what is most interesting for me in this whole scenario is we are looking with
hope at Shakti Kant Das Ji. He used to first manage the monetary policy. Now, he
is going to be here managing the fiscal policy. So, we have a domain expert. So, I
would really like to flag and ask the hon. Finance Minister of India what
intervention is he doing and we would want him to intervene because it is very
critical for our monetary and fiscal policy right now. More so, because RBI is
talking about this gold loan. So, it is a very important point and it is going to hurt
the economy if we do not get our act together on it. So, I request the hon. Finance
Minister to be very very concerned and make sure they take some action and be
prepared for this because the outcomes will hurt the country.
The other big issue which is raised several times is the FRBM Act. The
fiscal management is what this Bill is all about and the hon. Prime Minister of
India Shri Atal Behari Vajpayee Ji got this Bill, which is one of the finest Bills, in
24.03.2025 1027
the larger interest of the nation and the States, and Dr. Manmohan Singh Ji
continued it and improved it. Now, what I want to flag about this is that in the
FRBM Act, we do fiscal management. So, we make sure to see the Government
does not overspend. I will give you an example of my State. To make populist
programmes, I am putting it as diplomatically and correct as I can, that these are
all wonderful schemes for the larger interest of the nation which I appreciate. I like
it. I support it.
But now they have taken all the Adivasi funds, and the Department of
Social Justice funds are used for populist programmes. There is nothing wrong
with it. Every Government wants populist things. However, I remember the hon.
Prime Minister, who is clear about his economics, talked about rewaris at one
time. My question, with full humility to the hon. Finance Minister, is that after this,
what role or what checks and balances is this Government going to take so that
the budget is well spent and the monies are spent for the Adivasis and the Social
Justice Department, which are used for other schemes only during elections? So,
what happens to the Adivasis, and what happens to the Department of Social
Justice is my question.
Is this Government going to intervene for good management of fiscal deficit
and good governance, which this Government talks about? Are they going to walk
the talk for every State that gets into an election? They will be changing the rules
of the game, which will not hurt us right now, but you will have a legacy of issues
that generations will not forgive us for, just for winning one election.
Another important question is about GST. Shri Vijay Kelkar, a proud
24.03.2025 1028
Maharashtrian as I am, we both come from the same State, a tall leader, a
visionary, brought GST to this country. What was the idea behind it? The sole
idea of the GST was ‘one nation, one tax’. The hon. Finance Minister, in her
Budget speech, as a matter of fact, mentioned that they are going to reduce the
slabs. Well, the nation is waiting with bated breath because from three per cent all
the way up to 28 per cent, there are so many slabs, and there is so much
confusion.
I am not getting into the popcorn mess my children have to deal with. Let
them decide whether they want to eat salted or caramel or plain popcorn. I do not
want to get into that because I do not want to make this into a frivolous debate.
This is a very serious debate for me. But my point to this Government is: why is
there so much fear all the time in the GST when it was a commitment made?
The Finance Minister praised all the State Governments that the state
Governments are doing very well. So, I want to ask her one thing: when will we
become ‘one nation, one tax’, which is the real soul of this? All the developed
nations are doing that. Nishikant Dubey ji said their Government is going to be
number one in the world in doing this. We will be very happy if this Government
becomes the first country in the world. We will also be proud. ‘िवकिसत भारत’ म(cid:164) हम
भी आपका साथ द(cid:164)गे ।
But to reach there, what are you taking from the western world? I also want
to ask this. The good practices also have to be taken. Like all the other
developing countries have done, when is this Government going to do ‘one
nation, one tax’? I want to ask them this question. GST needs a lot of reforms,
24.03.2025 1029
which are suggested by many of us, and many of my colleagues have talked
about it.
I would like to ask this Government about tariffs. Tariffs are a big concern
for all of us. I want a clear-cut reply on unilateral zero tariff for agri-products. We
do not want to hear some long-winded story of what happened in the last 50
years. I want to know the stand of this Government on the unilateral zero tariff for
agri-products. Are they going to agree to this? Are they disagreeing? If it is no,
they have to tell this nation that there will be no tariff on agri-products; we will not
compromise with this for the farmers of this nation, and the policy will remain the
same. They have had discussions in America on this. We want to know about it.
I have two small points. They are about taxation system. We are coming up
with a new law. I am fortunately a part of the Select Committee. We are having
very, very good debates and discussions, and we are calling all the stakeholders.
There is one small point I would like to make. Everybody here in this country
wants to pay tax. We are all paying taxes. Why is there always a fear about this
tax? Most of my colleagues also spoke about it. I am not raising doubt on the
intention of this government. But why does this Government like to scare people?
Even in this new Bill, they are saying that we are not going into people’s mobiles
and personal spaces. My only point is that they have to put checks and balances
on everything. I do not want to get into whether high networths left or whether 22
per cent left.
If India really wants to grow, we should have a good tax regime and get
fear out of people. People want to stay in India; people want to build India, but
24.03.2025 1030
nobody wants to stay in fear. The whole point is, whenever you are going into
people’s spaces or taxing people, have trust. What is the status of the Vivad Se
Vishwas Scheme? Crores of rupees are stuck. You should bring the Vivad Se
Vishwas Scheme for everybody. You should extend that Scheme and show trust
in people. Indians are very honest people by nature. That is why we do globally
extraordinarily well. So, this Government has to stop this. You are sitting here in
Government because Indians have voted for you. But you want to tax them, scare
them, and make them fearful. It is their money only on which your election goes
on.
िजस थाली म(cid:164) खाते हो, उस नमक का थोड़ा तो (cid:197)यान रखो । I request the Government
to stop hurting people. People in India are honest. They are happy to pay tax.
Please do not scare them, and have a very transparent and a fair regime.
Thank you.

SHRI P. V. MIDHUN REDDY (RAJAMPET): Sir, I would like to recall that in the
Andhra Pradesh Reorganization Act, Section 90 clearly states that the Polavaram
project will be taken up as a national project, and all the necessary clearances
and land acquisition will be done by the Central Government. Recently, the
Department of Water Resources under the Ministry of Jal Shakti has given an
estimate which is pegged at Rs. 60,000 crore. In the recent budget, we have seen
that the height of the project or the storage level is being pegged at 41.15 metres
instead of the originally contemplated level of 45.72 metres. This is a great
injustice to our State. Furthermore, it was designed for 194 TMC, and if this is
going to happen, it will be restricted to 115 TMC, making it just a water retaining
structure, not a dam. I would like to state that 7.5 lakh acres of land is the
contemplated ayacut, and it is going to stabilize 23.5 lakh acres of farmland. If the
project is pegged at only 115 TMC, it is a great loss to the State.
Sir, through you, I would like to ask this from the Government. What is the
Government planning to do by reducing the capacity of the project? There have
been various statements, and the Government has to clarify whether they are
going to reduce the capacity of the project or restrict the amount being given to
the State. Instead of Rs. 60,000 crore, if only Rs. 30,000 crore is given to the
State, which was promised on this very floor of the House, it is a great loss to
24.03.2025 1039
Andhra Pradesh. None of the Telugu Desam Party Members have spoken on
this. The YSRCP has been demanding this. We want an explanation from the
Government. We want the Government to clarify that there is no loss to Andhra
Pradesh.
Additionally, regarding the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant, a Minister has
recently made contradictory statements. One person said that they are going to
support it with money and fund it, while another Minister has stated that they are
going to proceed with the privatization of the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant. We
want the Government to clarify this; we are not going to accept the privatization of
the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant. Many people have lost their lives to achieve the
Visakhapatnam Steel Plant, and if it is being privatized, it is a loss to the
employees and the State.
Moreover, the alliance of BJP, TDP, and Jana Sena is present in our State,
and whatever good schemes the previous Government implemented are being
neglected in a huge way in this current regime. For example, there is a scheme
for fees reimbursement that was started by late Y. S. Rajasekhara Reddy garu
and our former CM Jaganmohan Reddy ji.
17.04 hrs (Shri Jagdambika Pal in the Chair)
It is a very noble scheme that allows any child to study for free in any
engineering college or pursue higher education, with the Government covering all
their expenses. There are almost 11 lakh students who benefit from this every
year. However, in the past few quarters, the fees have not been paid by the
Government to the colleges, and the students are in great distress. They are
24.03.2025 1040
being asked to pay the money from their own pockets to enter the examination
hall. Many incidents have occurred, and our students have openly expressed their
concerns, but the Government is not acting. The Government should keep its
priorities clear – one is education, and the other is healthcare. If the Government
is unable to support the education of poor people, how is the State going to
prosper?
Imagine somebody getting Rs. 25,000 salary. How is he going to pay lakhs
and lakhs suddenly for his son's education? Imagine somebody getting Rs.
30,000 salary per month and he has a family of four. How is he going to support
them? The Government should act immediately. Through you, I would request the
Finance Minister to put some sense into the State Government’s head.
There, the former Chief Minister had introduced Arogyasri. Through this,
anybody can get treatment in any notified hospitals in the State. The payments to
these hospitals have not been made and the network hospitals have stopped
giving treatment to Arogyasri patients. This is a huge thing. A lot of lives are being
lost. As the people are not able to pay even Rs. 75,000, they are losing their
lives. These are very important things, and the Government should act on this.
The Central Government should tell the State Government, which is part of the
coalition in the State?
AP has got a dubious record. It is on the top in the price of diesel and petrol
in the country. Imagine Rs. 108.30 is the cost of diesel in Andhra Pradesh and in
the bordering State of Tamil Nadu it is just Rs. 100 and in Karnataka also it is Rs.
102. There is a huge difference of Rs. 8. Why will industries come to Andhra
24.03.2025 1041
Pradesh? Why will somebody want to invest there when it is so costly as
compared to our neighbouring States? I would request, through you, and I also
request the Finance Minister to put some pressure and reduce the cost of diesel,
which is so high in the State.
In one way, it is clearly evident that we are losing money for the Polavaram
Project and we want to know the reason for it. ‘Special Category’ status was a
promise for Andhra Pradesh, and they said a package will be given. What is this
package? It is a sort of illusion, and it is an imaginary package. We want the
Government to spell out what package was given and what benefit AP has got.
We are losing in everything. A lot of things that were promised in the AP
Reorganisation Act have not been fulfilled. The time of the AP Reorganization Act
has also lapsed. So, we want the Government to keep up its promise whatever
have been promised for Andhra Pradesh.
I would like to bring to the notice of the House that we appreciate the
Government for having given money for development of Amaravati as capital. We
appreciate it. But what is happening at the ground level also is a very important
thing. A kilometre of National Highways costs Rs. 20 crore in the same district.
The National Highways has called for tenders and people have bid for Rs. 20
crore, and here, the estimate has been jacked up from Rs. 40 crore to Rs. 50
crore. It is a pure waste of money. I wish the Central Government appointed
some officer who goes through all these estimates. A lot of money is being
misused and thousands of crores of money is being wasted.
We just need Rs. 1,500 crore to Rs. 2,000 crore to put the 17 new medical
24.03.2025 1042
colleges on track. They have stopped what the previous Government had taken
up. All the 17 medical colleges have been put on hold, and the worst part is that
they were ready to start it. They had taken the employees and they had even
allotted the seats. The same Government had written asking to take back the
seats as they were not able to run the colleges. On the one hand, they are
swindling money in this big way, and on the other hand they are not able to put in
Rs. 2,000 crore for the medical colleges. It is really in a bad state. Whatever good
things the previous Governments have done should be continued. …
(Interruptions)
HON. CHAIRPERSON : You are raising all the State subjects.
… (Interruptions)
SHRI P. V. MIDHUN REDDY : Sir, the Central Government has given money for
Amaravati. The Central Government has partially given money for a few medical
colleges, and they have been put on hold. They want to sell them to private
people, which we are demanding that it should not happen. We are also
demanding that the Central Government should step in and help in running these
colleges. We do not want them to be put idle. We do not want poor people to be
at a loss because 17 medical colleges mean 17 hospitals also where poor people
can get treatment.
We ask questions like these, and the Telugu Desam people try to put mud
on us. They are just going on putting allegations against us. They are booking
cases against us. We are not going to budge. We are going to question. If you
see, for almost a year, not even one thing relating to the State has been raised by
24.03.2025 1043
the TDP people. The TDP people do not have the guts to raise Polavaram issue
in the House. They do not have the guts to raise the issue about steel plant. The
only thing that they are doing is putting mud on us. The only thing they are doing
is, they are making points with no facts at all. This is baseless. I request the

SHRI E. T. MOHAMMED BASHEER (MALAPPURAM): Sir, thank you very
much.
Sir, this Finance Bill covers various relevant changes in the Income Tax
regime such as, compliance mechanism and expediting that; similarly, reduction
on certain Customs Duty; Income Tax exemption for start-ups; tax exemption for
 Expunged as ordered by the Chair.
24.03.2025 1053
IFSC; and NGO’s tax exemption for increased period under 12A. There are some
healthcare kinds of reliefs also.
Sir, I am of this strong opinion that whether it is a Finance Bill or any other
legislation, it must have a compassionate touch. In the light of that, I wish to point
out certain humanitarian kind of considerations, the disabled cases.
17.33 hrs (Shri Dilip Saikia in the Chair)
Sir, the cochlear implant, which is for the hearing-impaired persons, is
exempted. But at the same time, spare parts and accessories, have not been
exempted. So, that is to be included. It is a small thing. But it is a great thing. It
has to be done in that way. I request that kind of a tax exemption should be given
for that.
Sir, in this Budget, there is an exemption of customs duty on 36 life-saving
drugs, including drugs for cancer and rare diseases, and five per cent for other
treatments. Of course, it is welcome. It is because cancer treatment is a very
expensive treatment. And whatever may be the considerations that we are giving,
it will be a great thing. But we have to do more things.
Similarly, the Government is saying that they will start cancer centres in all
the districts. I humbly suggest that we must have a time-bound programme to get
it materialised. That is a very important thing. A time-bound action plan is also
required.
Sir, I would like to point out one more thing. Throughout the world, for the
physically-challenged persons, there are modern devices and equipment.
Unfortunately, in India, that has not been developed like that. We have to
24.03.2025 1054
emphasize it. Ill-fated persons are suffering like anything. Now, import
dependency is there. We can follow two methods.
Number one, this import duty can be changed in many ways. Similarly, as
far as Indian situation is concerned, we must have our own institutions for
manufacturing of these kinds of devices and equipment.
Coming to other things, I would like to say one thing. We as MPs get
hundreds of applications for help from PMNRF – Prime Minister’s National Relief
Fund. Poor cancer patients, kidney patients and many more patients are coming.
But the allotment to this PMNRF is negligible, very little. What I am suggesting is
this. Do a great thing. By enlarging the scope of PMNRF, we can give more help
to the cancer patients and like that. It is a normal thing. If we can do it, it will
surely be a very important thing. So, allotment in that thing may kindly be
enhanced.
Then, I come to the Dispute Redressal Mechanism in the tax side. We are
making a lot of reforms. It is quite natural that we will have some disputes but
dispute redressal mechanism is very poor. It takes a long time. So, I suggest that
very effective steps are to be taken for that also. So, I have another thing. Make
the tax payer happy to do it. In foreign countries, a lot of encouragements are
given in the form of concessions. Here also, it should be designed in such a
pattern that these tax payers will happily pay the tax. That kind of reform is also
very important in this.
Similarly, Sir, I would like to say this about FCRA – Foreign Contribution
Regulation Act. It is a very good Act. We are getting a lot of assistance from
24.03.2025 1055
different countries. We were doing very good work, wonderful work. What
happened now? They have in bulk cancelled the FCRA registrations. Kindly
explain why you are doing this cruelty. Is there any base for that? This FCRA
registration holding persons are not even aware on what ground this has been
cancelled. There is a total cancellation. So, I am telling you that these kinds of
things should not be done. So, you have to reconsider that point in the best
interest of humanity. That is what, I would like to say.
I have a simple issue. Worldwide, there is interest free banking system. In
Gulf countries and Arabian countries, they call it Islamic banking. In European
countries also, this kind of banking system is developing like anything. I am
suggesting that India should seriously think about it. In 2008, the Raghuram
Rajan Committee on Financial Sector Reforms suggested the introduction of
interest free banking in India. There are many other recommendations also. Why
India is allergic to this kind of thing? If there is any kind of Islamic banking, you do
it in a different way because interest free banking system is having high scope. I
suggest that also may be under your serious consideration. You should not have
any kind of allergic situation in these things.
With these few suggestions, I will stop here, Sir.
Thank you very much.

is all ... . If you really ask, India has not yet reached 5 trillion dollars. China has

Sir, the main problem facing the economy of the world today is Mr. Donald

not have that vision of Dr. Manmohan Singh.

SHRI MALAIYARASAN D. (KALLAKURICHI): Hon. Chairperson, Sir,
Vanakkam. Thank you for this opportunity. The Budget should analyse whether
the funds allocated for certain programmes are insufficient and it should clarify
after raising questions on the same. I am like a person who is hungry and in front
of whom just a banana leaf was spread and it is empty with nothing served on it.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
24.03.2025 1065
But to my neighbours sitting by my side, it was a feast served on the same
banana leaves. As regards the Union Budget, I am just like a hungry person from
Tamil Nadu with the same mentality looking at my neighbour who was served a
feast. This Budget which highlights the financial path of this Government, ignores
the real and genuine needs of States like Tamil Nadu. Destroying the Fiscal
federalism.
Hon. Chairperson Sir, when the State Budget of Tamil Nadu was presented
under the able guidance of Hon Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu Thalapathiyar, many
newspapers praised the State Budget as the one keeping in mind the needs of all
regions and all sections of the people. But the recent trends of this Union Budget
seem uneven and biased with a step motherly treatment. Saint Thiruvalluvar
guides the entire world as a political guru with the following couplet of Tirukkural.
“IyatRalum, Eettalum, Kaathalum, Kaaththa Vakuththalum Vallathu Arasu”. The
meaning of this couplet is that “A good Government is one which is well versed in
finding the ways of earning revenue, then to acquire wealth, protect the wealth
thus earned, and then distribute them wisely among each of the constituents.
Entire universe knows that our Hon Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu Thalapathiyar
follows this in true spirit.
Devolution of funds to Tamil Nadu has seen a sharp decline. As per the 9th
Finance Commission, the devolution percentage was at 7.831 per cent. But the
same has now come down to 4.079 per cent during the 15th Finance Commission.
Yes, it has come down to just 4.079 per cent. This has been a matter of shock for
us. Not only that, during January 2025, Union Government has made a devolution
24.03.2025 1066
of Rs 1,73,030 crore to all the States and out of which Tamil Nadu got only Rs 7,
057. 89 crore. Whereas Uttar Pradesh got a huge amount of Rs 31, 039.84 crore.
This is a matter of great concern. On the basis of this gap, if you see, it is similar
to the case of punishing an outstanding student of a class without teaching him
the lessons. This system of centralisation of powers with Union Government has
been strongly criticised by the political experts. Thiru K. Santhanam said that it
would be similar to the case of the States begging at the doorsteps of the Union
Government as beggars. Thiru Viswanatha Das, another expert said that it would
be similar to the case of States being treated like the destitute children standing
outside the North Block of the Union Government. These statements have
become reality in this Finance Bill.
Coming to GST compensation, Hon. Chairperson Sir, GST was introduced
with the assurances of compensating for the revenue losses incurred by the
States. Since the compensation period ended, States are facing challenges due
to financial instability. Tamil Nadu best known for robust production and services
sectors is worst affected. Tamil Nadu and other States, affected by the uncertain
financial crisis created by the Covid-19 pandemic, sought extension of GST
Compensation Cess beyond 30 June 2022. Even though this Compensation was
later extended up to the year 2026, the Union Government is only benefitted by
extra funds. But there is no system created for providing compensation to the
States. Moreover, the Union Government has not extended this. Due to this,
every year, Tamil Nadu is estimated to incur a loss to the tune of Rs 20, 000
crore. Sir, I want to say through you that our Hon. Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu
24.03.2025 1067
Thalapathi Thiru M.K. Stalin has time and again been raising this issue of loss to
the State of Tamil Nadu to the tune of Rs 20, 000 crore.
Coming to the sufferings of the farmers, agriculture is the backbone of our
country. Farmers of Tamil Nadu are facing several challenges particularly of
nature which is increasing day by day. Increased expenditure and impact of
climate change have affected their financial situation very badly. But I wish to say
that this Finance Bill has no concrete measure for supporting agriculture. It is a
matter of concern that the allocations to the crop insurance schemes are being
reduced. Farmers face erratic climatic conditions without adequate insurance
cover. The insurance claims paid under the Prime Minister Fasal Bima Yojana for
the year 2017-18 stood at Rs 29000 crore. This was further reduced to Rs 16,000
crore during 2022-23. Less allocation would mean less number of settlement of
claims being paid to farmers. Many farmers will be entangled in financial crisis.
Small farmers are forced to avail loans at exorbitant rates of interests from private
money-lenders. This will make farmers getting into the debt-ridden trap. Even
though the Union Government does not pay heed to the sufferings of farmers, the
State Government of Tamil Nadu under the leadership of Hon. Chief Minister of
Tamil Nadu Thiru M.K. Stalin, has waived off farm loans to the tune of Rs 12, 110
crore in the Tamil Nadu State Cooperative Banks. Not only that, everybody is
aware of the fact that the Tamil Nadu government has been providing life to
farmers by extending interest-free loans to the tune of Rs 54, 800 crore during the
years 2021 to 2025.
Infrastructure and its state of affairs are being ignored. Infrastructure is the
24.03.2025 1068
backbone of economic development. But important schemes of Tamil Nadu were
ignored without allocation of funds from the Union. Only after prolonged agitation
for almost three years consecutively, funds have been released for expansion of
Chennai Metro. As regards the construction of AIIMS at Madurai, only foundation
stone was laid and no other progress was made in this regard. I wish to state that
AIIMS has not been put to use for the general public as the work is being delayed
unnecessarily. But I want to say that our Hon. Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu has
created Kalaignar Centenary 1000-bedded hospital within a span of just 15
months and all the people are getting benefits from this hospital. It is one step
backward if we try to minimize the social benefits.
Hon Chairperson Sir, Tamil Nadu, under the able leadership of our Hon.
Chief Minister, is in the forefront of our country as regards ensuring education,
health and social security. I wish to state that for Mahatma Gandhi National
Employment Guarantee Scheme, MGNREGA, is aimed at supporting a large
chunk of people. There is either a reduced allocation in the Finance Bill for
MGNREGA, Midday meal scheme and other Schemes meant for marginalised
sections of the Society. This reduced allocation or an inordinate delay in release
of funds continue to be a threat and will be putting our several decades of growth
into the backseat. Hon Chairperson Sir, Tamil Nadu Government has always
been in the forefront in the implementation of MGNREGA. Tamil Nadu always
excels in the implementation of MGNREGA as regards creation of man days,
participation of women, providing work for the differently-abled, timely payment of
wages, timely completion of work and asset creation. As many as 29.79 crore
24.03.2025 1069
man days of wage employment have been achieved for the year 2024-24 up to
10.3.2025. The proposal for revising this labour Budget for Tamil Nadu with 35
crore man days of wage employment has been sent to the Union Ministry of Rural
Development. Similarly, no fund has been released under MGNREGA to Tamil
Nadu up to 10.3.2025. As much as Rs 2, 830 crore is pending dues for Tamil
Nadu as against the payment to be given to workers under MNREGA. I urge upon
the Union Government to pay these dues for Tamil Nadu immediately. Besides,
funds worth Rs 2172 crore is pending from the Union for education in Tamil Nadu.
An amount of Rs 3,000 crore is pending for release to Tamil Nadu as regards Jal
Shakti Schemes.
I am duty-bound to say that Tamil Nadu government has been ignored by
the Union Government. Tamil Nadu is in the forefront in taking forward the
development of our country. States like Tamil Nadu are contributing a lot for
raising the image of India in the entire world. This House should ponder over the
fact that what would happen if States like Tamil Nadu are weakened by the
Union. It is our farsighted vision to make our federal system stronger thereby
ensuring prosperity for every State of this country and development to its every
citizen. It is the duty of this august House to ensure that our financial policy
reflects all these goals besides developing unity in diversity. Thank you. Tamil
Nadu will fight for its rights; Tamil Nadu will win. Thank you.

24.03.2025 1072
DR. M. K. VISHNU PRASAD (CUDDALORE): Thank you, hon. Chairperson, for
allowing me to speak on the Finance Bill.
We all know India is the fifth largest economy in the world, next to the US,
China, Germany, and Japan. But the major problem is, though in this Budget we
had a lot of tax cuts, there are no long-term reforms. In a nutshell, India faces
challenges like food inflation, unemployment, stagnation in growth, and trade
deficits.
In 2047, India may become a developed nation. That is what we are all
saying. But to achieve that, we should have an 8 per cent growth rate. But right
now, we are having only 6.3 per cent growth rate. With 600 million labour force
and a rapidly expanding middle-class, the strategically geopolitical position of
India is attracting a lot of foreign investments like magnet. But what is worrying for
India is that we have Rs.181 lakh crore debt which is 57.3 per cent of GDP.
According to the Finance Commission, there has to be 40 per cent cap on GDP.
This is a very alarming state. The per capita GDP growth from 2004 to 2014 is
USD 624 to USD 1560. That is almost 250 per cent. But from 2014 to 2023, it is
only 160 per cent that is, USD 1560 to USD 2485, which is a steep fall in the
growth.
In 2023-24, our hon. Finance Minister said in this August House that the
farmers are vital for our nation. They are like eyes, ears and hearts of this
country. But unfortunately, in this Budget, for agriculture sector, the allocation is
only 2.7 per cent, that is, Rs.136 lakh crore. It is a very sad state of affairs for the
agriculture of our country.
24.03.2025 1073
In 2014, when Mr. Narendra Modi was announced as a future Prime
Minister, his first promise to the nation was that if he becomes the Prime Minister
of the country, definitely he would implement the recommendations of the
Swaminathan Commission. But till now it is not implemented. This also has to be
taken very seriously in our account.
There is one more thing, Sir. Our hon. Prime Minister took one step which
is poisonous for the farmers. He formulated nano-fertilizer. Nano-fertilizer is a big
failure as per the record. The yield per acre is very, very low and the farmers are
committing suicide day by day. Another important point is the gold loan. Normally,
the farmers, small and poor labourers, and small traders go for gold loan. The
main point is, for one year they have a loan. If they cannot pay back, if they
cannot close the loan, they can renew the loan by just paying the interest. But
now, this Government has changed the rule. They said, `if you want to renew the
loan, you have to pay the principal and the interest. After paying them, then you
can renew the loan.’ If the poor farmer has that kind of money with him for paying
the principal and the interest, then why he has to go for a loan? During the UPA
regime, Dr. Manmohan Singh Ji and Madam Sonia Ji gave six per cent tax
deduction for agri-gold loan. But this Government has cancelled it.
Most importantly, in the name of delimitation, we have been deprived of our
rights. Now itself, the MPs from South India are limited in numbers, so we
become irrelevant. When our numbers keep reducing based on your statement,
then we will become totally irrelevant. Then the voice of the minority will be
brutally crushed by the noise of the majority. This should not be accepted.
24.03.2025 1074
Sir, India is a federal State. The Prime Minister and this Union Government
should look at the States as a father and mother, like parents to the children.
Every State is important. But unfortunately, Gujarat is having Rs. 16,000
allocations for railway, but Tamil Nadu has got only Rs. 6,000 crore for railway. In
Gujarat, recently a project worth Rs. 50,000 crore for developing a port is taking
place. But in Tamil Nadu, in Cuddalore, my Constituency, only Rs. 150 crore was
given for the Sagarmala project. But till now, there is no work happening in my
Constituency.
Sir, out of 19.36 crore houses, only 15.53 crore tap connections have been
given, which is only 80.2 per cent.
Now, I move on to the National Education Policy. We are sending rockets.
In ISRO, mainly Tamils, Malayalis, Kannadigas, and Telugu people are working.
They are having that kind of rocket science. They did not get education under the
NEP before. We are very, very far high in our education policies. Our standards
are very high. We look at other States like our brothers. We wanted all to come
with our pace. But we do not want to grab anybody's space. It is not at all fair to
show this NEP and deprive us of our funds. There are broken classrooms. Poor
students are suffering. Can this Government not approach them with a motherly
vision? Why do you have to threaten? Why do you have to blackmail? You are
saying, `sign this, accept the NEP, then only will we give the funds of Rs. 2,500
crore.’
The Indian Meteorological Department is again a big failure. There are so
many cyclones; there is so much of disaster; there is so much of catastrophes;
24.03.2025 1075
but nothing is happening. When we ask for funds, this Government is turning a
blind eye.
I will come to some technical points. There are personal income tax rates.
There is a substantial increase in the personal income tax slabs and rates of tax.
It is a welcome move. But regarding what was mentioned in the Speech, has it
been translated into the Bill? The answer is `no’. Instead of increasing the
threshold limit, the Government has increased the tax rebate under Section 87A.
In the public mind there is a doubt that if there is less tax collection in the
following year, whether this rebate would stand reduced. When a Government is
giving an exemption, it must graciously give the same without attaching any frills
to it. The best way would have been to increase the threshold limit and keep the
tax working simple.
Secondly, there are now two sets of tax regime. Why are we following two
sets of tax regime? Only one slab of taxation would be effective and it must be
simpler to a common man. In the new regime, only a standard deduction of Rs.
75,000 is allowed. The main thing is this. In the case of construction of new
houses and education, that is not to be considered under the regular deduction.
This has to be considered seriously. The logic behind reducing the rate is said to
be that the public would have more disposable income, and they will use the
same for spending. Once they spend even 30 per cent of the disposable income,
it will bring in GST of 18 per cent on it. The logic is good, but is it right for the
Indian public? That is a moot point. All these years, the Indian middle-class has
been saving their hard-earned money.
24.03.2025 1076
Now the Government wants them to be spendthrift and to suffer when they
are not earning at all. Mainly in the MSME sector, the Government had inserted,
through the Finance Act 2023, a new sub-section 43 B (h) in the Act to ensure
timely payments of dues to the micro and small sector. Assuming that a micro unit
sells a product, even if it gives say 60 days’ time to the customer to pay, the Act
mandates that within 45 days the payment is to be made, otherwise the
expenditure will not be allowed as deduction. This has created issues for the
micro and small sector with their customers informing them that unless they
deregister from the MSMED Act, no further orders would be given, or they would
give orders to other non-micro or small sector suppliers.
Most importantly, TDS continues to be tedious for a common man. There is
no easy mechanism. Much of the time of the accounts staff of the entities is going
into this. They have to verify the TDS rate, limit, get the PAN number, and verify
whether it is linked to Aadhaar. Further, there is still a two-leg process. First, there
is payment. After a quarter, a return has to be filed. If the return is not filed on
due date, a penalty of Rs.200 per day is levied. There should be one challan
payment every month with all the details, and no further return is to be mandated.
The Finance Bill, 2025 introduces a retrospective tax amendment that
overturns past court rulings and imposes unexpected tax liability on business.
The proposed amendment to Section 17(5)(d) of the CGST Act denies input tax
credit. It is against the law. Even the Odisha Government has given a ruling. This
has to be taken seriously.
Last but not least, the Finance Ministry is otherwise called as FM. FM
24.03.2025 1077

means Finance Ministry. Now the common man, the people say that FM is ...
Ministry because everything is done always in wonderland. There is always talk,
only announcement. In reality, on the ground, nothing is happening. It is big
zero.
Thank you very much, Sir. I strongly oppose this Bill.

DR. THOL THIRUMAAVALAVAN (CHIDAMBARAM): Hon Chairman Sir,
Vanakkam. Fiscal Responsibility Budget Management Act was legislated during
the regime of our former Prime Minister Shri Atal Bihari Vajpayee. As per this Act,
a target was set to reduce our debt burden to 38.7 per cent of our total Gross
Domestic Product, GDP during the year 2022-23. But when this BJP Government
came to power under the leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi, this debt
burden was at 51.1 per cent. Now this debt burden has increased to 57 per cent
of the GDP. This proves that the Union Government has failed in managing the
economic affairs of this country. As much as 37 per cent of the gross revenue is
being spent to repay the interest accumulated on our debts. This Government is
struggling to repay its debts and thereby unable to allocate adequate funds for the
developmental programmes. This Union Government is therefore unable to
allocate funds for the States as well.
Funds meant for Education Department of Tamil Nadu have not been
released. Disaster relief fund was not provided for Tamil Nadu. Funds meant for
100-day employment guarantee scheme were not allocated. As per the New
Educational Policy 6 per cent is the fund that should be allocated for education in
the country. This was the norm set by this present Government. But the
Government is struggling with debts and is unable to allocate even 3 per cent
funds for education. Similarly, the Government is unable to allocate funds for
health and as a result of which Swachh Bharat Cleanliness programmes have
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
24.03.2025 1085
failed completely. Nobody can deny this.
Jal Jeevan Mission is an ambitious Scheme of this Government for
providing drinking water for all. This scheme welcomed by all sections of people
is not even funded with 50 per cent of the assured allocation. That is the worst
state of financial affairs in the country. The debt burden on GDP has increased
and I urge that the Union Government should accept its failure in this august
House.
I have one more important demand to make before I conclude my speech.
Buddhist monks are into indefinite hunger strike. This is a demand from their side
even before the time of Indian independence. This Bodhgaya Temple was
constructed during the period of King Ashoka. This is the place where our Lord
Buddha got enlightenment. Not only Buddhists from Bihar but Buddhists from all
over the world visit Bodh Gaya, the sacred place. There is Bodh Gaya Temple
Management Committee set up under the Bodh Gaya Temple Act. There are
more number of non-Buddhists in this Management Committee. There are no
Buddhists in this Committee. I urge upon this Government to hand over the
management of this Bodh Gaya temple to the Buddhist community.)
I would like to raise here some demands. Transfer the management of the
Maha Bodhi Temple to the Buddhist community. Second, amend the Bodh Gaya
Temple Act of 1949 to ensure that the Temple Management Committee is
exclusively composed of Buddhist members. Third demand, restrict the conduct
of non-Buddhist religious ceremonies within the premises of Maha Bodhi Temple.
This is very essential and a sensitive issue. So, I request our Union Government
24.03.2025 1086
to intervene in this issue to restore Bodh Gaya Temple to Buddhists. Thank you.

SHRIMATI HARSIMRAT KAUR BADAL (BATHINDA): Thank you, Sir. I rise to
oppose this Budget because the Budget 2025-26 falls extremely short of
addressing several challenges and pressing economic concerns. I also oppose it
for the fact that in the last ten Budgets, the name of Punjab has not even been
mentioned by the hon. Finance Minister. It seems to me that Punjab does not
seem to exist for them in the map of India. I would say that this is extremely sad
that a State, which has contributed a lot to the nation, is not considered in the
country’s Budget despite it facing several issues.
Today, NITI Aayog’s Fiscal Health Index shows that Punjab has ranked
24.03.2025 1088
lowest amongst the 18 major Indian States, with a score of just 10.7, which is far
behind Odisha with a leading score of 67.8. Due to the present Government and
the previous Congress Government's mishandling of their expenditure, revenue
mobilization, debt management and the State exchequer, we have reached a
state of fiscal mismanagement, where today we are ranked lowest among the 18
major States of the country. Even though Bihar may be worse than us, this
Budget mentions it six times and allocates the lion's share of all the largest
Government resources, while Punjab, which faces all these issues, receives
nothing.
Sir, I agree that the economy has grown, but there is no doubt that it has
been a jobless growth. Today, the Indian youth account for almost 83 per cent of
the unemployed workforce. The young people with secondary and higher
education among the total unemployed Indians have risen from 35 per cent in
2002 to 65 per cent in 2022, which clearly shows that this Government's focus
towards job creation has not been successful or it is not in the agenda. What is
even worse off in this budget is that there is no strategy for the layoffs which are
happening due to computerization, artificial intelligence and other technological
advancements, be it in IT sector and be it in other sectors.
Sir, the MSME sector, which is the most important sector, and which is the
backbone of the country, contributes 30 per cent to our GDP and 50 per cent to
our exports. After agriculture, this is the second largest employment generating
sector. Despite 20 per cent of the investment, MSME provides employment to 80
per cent of the industrial workforce. Unfortunately, there is no focus on the MSME
24.03.2025 1089
sector in this Budget.
Then, there is a Productivity-Linked Incentive scheme. But this is only for
the big corporates which can put in big money. I have a suggestion to the
Government in this regard. Why does the Government not tweak the PLI scheme
to bring in those industries which generate more employment in the economy?
They do not have big money like the big corporates to be able to fund it, but they
actually generate a huge number of employment opportunities. So, why does the
Government not extend the PLI scheme to the small industries, be it the bicycle
factory of Ludhiana in my State, be it the sports goods industry, be it the brass
industry of Muradabad, be it any of the hand tools industry and all these small
industries which generate jobs. So, the PLI scheme should be extended to those
industries, where they can increase revenues, increase exports and provide
employment. This is my suggestion to the Government. But instead of looking at it
this way, it is unfortunate that this Budget has no allocation for these industries.
For example, regarding bicycles, it has cut import duties from 35 per cent to 20
per cent. This is making our bicycle industry vulnerable to Chinese and
Taiwanese imports, which is what this Government should be safeguarding
against and provide incentives for this industry to upgrade itself.
Earlier, there was a scheme which was known as Credit Linked Capital
Subsidy Scheme (CLCSS), wherein MSMEs could upgrade their technology.
Unfortunately, this has been discontinued. So, the Government does not give
anything new to them but has discontinued what they had earlier. This way, the
Government disregards a huge segment which provides employment to hundreds
24.03.2025 1090
and thousands of people. I would urge the Government to do this immediately.
The Government's decision to increase the MSME turnover threshold from
Rs.250 crore to Rs. 500 crore is highly disproportionate. How many MSMEs
operate with a business model of Rs.200 crore, Rs.250 crore, or Rs.500 crore?
There is hardly one percent of MSMEs that will receive 95 per cent of these
benefits. Meanwhile, 99 per cent of MSMEs remain below that threshold limit and
will receive nothing. Therefore, I urge the Government to seriously reconsider this
matter.
Eventually, I am extremely sad that there was not a single mention about
the Minimum Support Price in this Budget. िपछले पांच साल(cid:332) से हमार े िकसान आंदोलन
कर रहे ह(cid:167) । वे मार खा रहे ह(cid:167), डंडे खा रहे ह,(cid:167) जेल(cid:332) म(cid:164) जा रहे ह(cid:167) । वे सद(cid:234)-गम(cid:234) म(cid:164) बैठ कर धरना दे रहे ह(cid:167) ।
जो एमएसपी का वादा िकया गया था, इस पूर े बजट म(cid:164) उसका िज(cid:248) तक नह(cid:233) ह ै । आपने वादा िकया
था, अब आप उससे भी पीछे मड़ु गए । म(cid:167) इस सरकार से कहना चाहती ह(cid:243)ं िक (cid:367)(cid:204)प सरकार जोर लगा
रही है िक हमारा ए(cid:250)ीक(cid:208)चर माक(cid:165) ट यहां पर खोल देना चािहए । वे अपने (cid:256)ू ट्स, वेिजटेब(cid:208)स, दूध, मीट

Government has not given in to the WTO pressure and we hope that this

The Finance Minister presented this Budget against the backdrop of
24.03.2025 1091
economic distress, slowing growth, rising inequality, stagnating wages, and a
decline in private investment. Yet, instead of addressing these pressing issues,
the Budget presents a mirage of progress, concealing deeper economic failures,
and policy missteps. The budgets are not just numbers; they reflect the
Government’s vision for a country. This Budget, unfortunately, presents a vision
that prioritizes fiscal consolidation and corporate appeasement over social
welfare and economic resilience. The Government’s narrative of economic
strength and inclusivity stands in stark contrast to the reality on the ground, where
millions continue to struggle with rising prices, unemployment, and stagnating
wages. This speech seeks to unpack the layers of deception in this Budget.
What is our state of the economy? The Government projected a nominal
GDP growth of 10.5 per cent, but the First Advanced Estimate (FAE) for 2024-25
is lower, at 9.8 per cent. The real GDP growth is expected to be between 6.5 per
cent and 7 per cent and has settled at 6.4 per cent, a decline from 8.2 per cent
the previous year. These numbers alone expose the Government’s over-
estimated claims and the fragile state of our economy. The economic growth in
isolation does not indicate prosperity. We must examine how the growth is
distributed across sectors and among different segments of our society. A closer
look at the growth sector trends reveals troubling signs.
Manufacturing, a critical driver of employment, has slowed down from 9.9
per cent to 5.3 per cent. Mining growth has plummeted from 7.1 per cent to 2.9
per cent. Trade and transport growth has dipped from 6.4 per cent to 5.8 per cent.
Bank credit growth has fallen drastically from Rs. 21 lakh crore to Rs. 11.5 lakh
24.03.2025 1092
crore. Cement production, again a core indicator of the infrastructure growth of
our country, has dropped from 10.3 per cent to 3.1 per cent. These indicators
make one thing abundantly clear. The Government’s claims of robust economic
growth are built on quicksand. The so-called high GDP growth does not translate
into higher employment opportunities or increased wages for workers. Instead,
the corporate profits are surging even as real wages for workers stagnate or
decline.
There is a clear divide when it comes to the matter of progress, especially
when compared to other statistics. We can see a clear divide between the North
and the South. We know that the Government is always famous for dividing the
country on the basis of religion, on the basis of food we eat, and on the basis of
clothes we wear. But this is the first time that even on the basis of GST, the
country is divided between North and South. I will give you a small example. The
traditional snacks we consume in Kerala, commonly known as banana fritters, are
emotionally significant for Malayalees. Pazham pori is a common snack in our
daily routine. Ulundu vada, dal vada, and all steamed items like kozhukatta and
ela ada, which are rice dumplings, are taxed at 18 per cent GST. On the other
hand, we can see that jalebi, laddu, burfi, and gulab jamun are taxed at five per
cent. Lord Ayyappa will not forgive the BJP or the ruling party.
जो (cid:255)साद लेता है, उ(cid:198)नी अ(cid:200)पम पर भी जीएसटी लगाया ह ै । Even the curse of Lord
Ayyappa is not going to make you happy. I am telling you that they are going to
be in big trouble with this taxation. This is a serious thing. We should not take it
lightly. According to the Kerala Bakers Association, the GST remedy was put
24.03.2025 1093
forward to them. A HSN code was the only issue. But it is not sorted out yet.
The GST on life saving drugs is an important issue. The very small and
young children, who suffer from the Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) disease, are
in a critical condition. There are thousands of young people across the country,
and the cost of the medicine is exorbitant. लाइफ-सेिवंग (cid:369)ग है । इसके ऊपर 5 परस(cid:164)ट
जीएसटी है, which makes it Rs. 80,00,000 for a single dose of medicine. These are
things which the Government has to just scrap. It is a basic issue. The GST
Council has to take a decision on this issue. Many other issues are in place.
When the Government talks about fishermen, they say a lot of things. But
unfortunately, the life and livelihood of fishermen are in great trouble. The
equipment that they use like the fishing ropes, nets, even the fishing hooks and
ice boxes that they use for their daily livelihood has been taxed at 18 per cent.
This is exorbitant. This has to be removed.
The Budget’s betrayal again continues. In the ongoing economic hardship
faced by millions, the Government has chosen to systematically reduce the
expenditure on critical sectors. Education spending fell short by Rs. 11,580 crore,
affecting millions of students who rely on the Government-funded schools and
scholarships. Food subsidy was slashed by Rs. 7,830 endangering food security
for the poorest sections of society. Rural development expenditure was cut down
by a staggering Rs. 75,133 crore further weakening the rural economy, which
employs a significant portion of our population. Health spending was reduced to
Rs. 1,255 crore at a time when the healthcare accessibility remains a major
concern after the post-pandemic. Agriculture and allied sector spending dropped
24.03.2025 1094
by Rs. 75,000 crore, ignoring the plight of farmers struggling with the rising input
cost and climatic change-related disruptions. Transfers for Centrally-sponsored
Schemes were reduced by Rs. 90,642 crore weakening grassroot welfare
programs.
Such drastic expenditure cuts in crucial sectors suggested that the
Government is prioritizing fiscal discipline over the welfare measures of our
citizens. This fiscal prudence should not come at the cost of basic human needs
such as food security, education and healthcare.
Who really benefits? I am concluding, Sir. Every year, the Government
proclaims tax relief for the middle-class. This time also, it is said so. In rural India,
the per capita monthly expenditure is Rs. 4,122 and in urban it is Rs. 6,996. The
vast majority of Indians are nowhere near the income threshold to pay income
tax. Only 2.8 crore people, which is just 4.6 per cent of the workforce, actually pay
income tax while the remaining 95 per cent struggle to make their ends meet. So,
who truly benefits from these tax cuts? It is certainly not the poor. …
(Interruptions)
I am concluding Sir. It is not even the majority of the middle-class. It is the
wealthy / elite people who reaps the rewards while the common citizen is
burdened with inflation, high indirect taxes and reduced Government support.
Sir, I strongly oppose the Finance Bill brought by the hon. Finance Minister.

Sir, I am just concluding. While I am opposing this, my last point is that
24.03.2025 1095
being a Keralite and being a Member of Parliament from Kerala, there are
significant numbers of NRIs who contribute to our economy. Unfortunately, an
exorbitant tax is imposed on them whenever they buy a property in our State or
whenever they have a transaction in our State. So, this exorbitant tax has to be

The NRIs make their livelihood. I strongly oppose the Bill.
DR. C. N. MANJUNATH (BANGALORE RURAL): Sir, I really congratulate Prime
Minister, Narendra Modi ji, who has strived hard to really rationalise and
democratise the tax reforms as well as tax policies. India is steadily marching
towards becoming the third largest economy. The scientific and synchronised
direct and indirect taxes are playing an important role in shaping our economy.
This year, the Budget has crossed the important landmark mathematically
of Rs. 50.65 lakh crore. So, in my opinion, Nirmala Sitharaman ji's Budget is a
golden jubilee Budget. NDA is the DNA of a progressive, performing, and
sustainable economy. It is the DNA of a positive economy. The Income Tax Act is
as old as 1961. Some of the important landmark decisions taken with regard to
this Finance Bill have tried to address some of the loopholes because this Act
was highly voluminous with a lot of provisions and chapters which have been
reduced by 35 to 40 per cent. It is now more taxpayer-centric and investor-centric.
We really congratulate Nirmala Sitharaman ji on this.
If you look at tax revenue, it has grown from 10.1 per cent to 13.1 per cent
in the last one year. People talk about the fiscal deficit and GDP. They are all
mathematical figures. What is important is that it is visible. Sir, 10 to 15 years
24.03.2025 1096
back, when we used to visit Europe and America, we would return home
disappointed hearing that India did not have world-class express highways, world-
class railway infrastructure or the airports. Today, it is visible right across the
country; we have got world-class express highways, world-class railways, world-
class airports, and seaports.
As far as tax devolution is concerned, the tax devolution to the States has
increased by 35 per cent. Yes, people talk about discrepancies in GST from north
to south, that is a different issue. They have also authorised the States to raise
Rs. 1.5 lakh crore of loan over 50 years’ guarantee to take care of the States’
needs.
It has already been discussed by fellow hon. Members regarding the tax
exemption limit up to Rs. 12 lakh, but how much money is put into circulation
because of this tax benefit? About Rs. 1 lakh crore has been put into circulation
by the Union Government. The second important component of this Bill is the
introduction of a unified tax system which aligns the financial year with the
assessment year as the tax year. Now, there is a very simple methodology of
filing the returns.
The third important thing is to give India's businessmen a chance to
participate in the international financial sector. They have widened the definition
of ‘digital asset’. There was a sort of tax terrorism in the previous Act. Now, they
have given authority to the officials to investigate the scams. But most
importantly, they have brought in a provision of decriminalisation in the Act. As a
result, the tax terrorism issue is virtually taken out.
24.03.2025 1097
As a result, more and more people will come forward to file the returns. Of
course, GST was a landmark decision taken in July 2017. In fact, it has widened
the tax base. Earlier, in GST, only about three crore people were in the return net;
now about 8.4 crore people are coming under the Income-tax net; so, that is a
healthy development.
Another important thing is that raw silk, raw jute and khadi have been
exempted from GST. I come from the sericulture valley or silk valley of India, that
is, Ramanagaram district in my constituency. India is the second largest producer
of silk, and Karnataka is ranked at first position in silk production. There are some
issues concerned with this industry. Nearly 6400 tonnes of silk waste is generated
and 90 per cent of it is exported. When it is exported, particularly to China, and
other countries, they make beautiful by-products and send back to us at
exorbitant prices. Of course, we have spinning mills where these by-products can
be made. These spinning mills need to be increased, particularly in my
constituency because Ramanagaram district in my constituency is considered to
be the silk city. We need to establish a silk museum and also a silk tourism centre
in this place. That is one of our demands.
I will come back to the other issues, particularly about tax deduction. The
TDS limit has been increased on senior citizens as well as on the rent. That is a
healthy development. We congratulate the Finance Minister. The Government
has also made some special provision for sunrise sectors. The sunrise sectors
also include electric vehicles and renewable energy. One important thing about
this sunrise sector is that the date for claiming the tax relief, that is zero tax, is
24.03.2025 1098
extended up to 31st July 2030 to promote the growth of all sunrise sectors,
whether it is renewable energy, information technology or electric vehicles. Even
for electric vehicles, for example, they are giving a subsidy for every kilometre. If
you convert the diesel vehicles into electric vehicles, they are giving a subsidy.
Another sunrise sector where the Government is giving a lot of subsidies is
semiconductor because for semiconductors, we are always looking at Russia or
at the United States. Now India is going well in the area of semiconductor, and
Rs.1.5 lakh crore has been invested in semiconductors.
The Ayushman Bharat scheme is the world's largest health assurance
scheme, where 60 crore people have been enrolled and eight crore procedures
have been done. As a result, the out-of-pocket expenditure has been decreased
from 62 per cent to 30 per cent, and under the national dialysis scheme, 25 lakh
patients are undergoing dialysis programme. That is a great thing. The hub-and-
spoke model for treatment of heart attack is also included, because treatment of
heart attack has to be taken to the doorsteps of Taluka hospitals, where initial
treatment is given in the form of a clot dissolving medicine. After stabilisation,
they are shifted to the nearest tertiary care centres.
There is a long-pending desire and dream of people of Karnataka to have
an AIIMS institution in the aspirational district of Raichur. I hope the hon. Prime
Minister, the Finance Minister and the Health Minister will take cognisance of this,
Finally, I thank you very much for giving me this time.
