SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Thank you, Madam Chairperson for
allowing me participate in the discussion on the Finance Bill, 2025.
The Finance Bill, 2025 is to give effect to the taxation proposals for the
Financial Year 2025-26. The gross tax revenue as per the Budget estimate for
2025-26 is Rs.42.70 lakh crore, which is 10.8 per cent more than that of the
revised estimate of 2024-25. The tax devolution to the States is Rs.14.22 lakh
crore. The net tax revenue of the Union Government is Rs.28.37 lakh crore. The
24.03.2025 1104
non-tax revenue is Rs.5,83,000 crore, which is 9.8 per cent increase than that of
the revised estimate of 2024-25.
I would like to seek certain clarifications from the hon. Finance Minister
regarding four issues after the present statistics.
The number one issue is this. How is the Government going to achieve the
target of 9.8 per cent increase in the non-tax revenue? Madam, in the last
financial year, the Reserve Bank of India has given one-time dividend of Rs. 2.11
lakh crore, or an increase of almost 47 per cent. This time such an allocation is
not there. So, how are you going to achieve the target? That is number one.
Madam, the second issue is this. The ratio of primary deficit to the fiscal
deficit is in a declining trend, which shows fiscal discipline. I do agree with that.
But at the same time, I would like to caution the Government that this fiscal
discipline is at the cost of economic growth, job creation, and essential public
services. So, my suggestion is that a gradual and strategic reduction in the
primary deficit, coupled with increased revenue generation, is the right thing to be
done by the Government.
The third point is regarding the income tax receipt. Madam Chairperson, in
2025-26, the Government is envisaging and estimating Rs. 14.38 lakh crore by
way of income tax, that is, 14.4 per cent increase over the Revised Estimates of
2024-25. Madam, it is illogical because already you have provided tax rebate for
an income less than Rs. 12 lakh. Further, there is no great income boosting
schemes to raise the income beyond Rs. 12 lakh. How come this 14.4 per cent
increase in the income tax receipt?
24.03.2025 1105
The fourth point is the illogical claim related to the Modified Interest
Subvention Scheme, that is, MISS. The limit on credit is enhanced from Rs. 3
lakh to Rs. 5 lakh but there is no increase in the Budget allocation with respect to
the same. It is same. It is Rs. 22,600 crore, which is same as that of the previous
Budget. So, how are you going to achieve that target?
These are the four clarifications which I would like to seek from the hon.
Finance Minister.
Now, I come to the Finance Bill. According to the hon. Finance Minister, the
reforms envisaged in the Finance Bill will simplify the tax procedure, boost
consumption, and stimulate private investment. But a critical evaluation of the
Finance Bill reveals that it may harm long-term fiscal sustainability, deepen
inequality and undermine essential public services.
Madam, lowering of the corporate tax is giving a wrong message. The
corporate giveaways or the concessions and incentives to corporates, is resulting
in the reduction of revenues available for public services and social welfare
programmes. The economic experts are saying that development is freedom only
when economic democracy is achieved, not when the rich are further enriched at
the cost of public welfare. And I can very well substantiate it. If you see the
income between Rs. 1 crore and Rs. 5 crore, there is an increase of 72 per cent
from 2021-22 to 2023-24; between Rs. 5 crore and Rs. 10 crore and between Rs.
10 crore to Rs. 25 crore, there is an increase of 72 per cent. And kindly see the
slab between Rs. 25 crore and Rs. 50 crore, there is an increase of 84 per cent
but from Rs. 50 crore to Rs. 100 crore, there is an increase of 102 per cent; from
24.03.2025 1106
Rs. 100 crore to Rs. 500 crore, there is an increase of 101 per cent; and above
Rs. 500 crore, there is an increase of 119 per cent in the number of taxpayers. It
shows that the higher income bracket is increasing but the per capita income is
not increasing. That means inequality is an alarming situation. That is to be
addressed. That is the point which I am speaking of.
Madam, I am skipping all other points. I am coming to a very important
point with regard to India’s growth as compared to the global economic growth.
With that point, I will conclude. According to the World Bank report in January,
2025, global economy is expected to have a steady growth at 2.7 per cent in
2025-26. However, the global economy appears to be settling at a lower growth
rate. That will be insufficient to foster sustained economic development.
The new US Administration led by Mr. Donald Trump has stated that global
trade war will reduce US growth as well as global growth in general. The growth
forecast of United States of America is down from 2.1 per cent to 1.7 per cent.
India’s forecast is down from 1.7 per cent to 1.5 per cent. These rates are below
three per cent of the annual average from the last year. So, the specific question
which I would like to ask the hon. Finance Minister is this. China has announced a
fiscal stimulus to boost the economic growth of China. And Mr. Donald Trump has
further cautioned, and he threatened, that from April 2nd onwards, that means
from the next week onwards, to impose reciprocal tariffs from April 2nd onwards.
So, our exports will be affected, and both the domestic demand and the economy
will be in trouble.
So, I would like to seek an answer from the hon. Minister how Indian
24.03.2025 1107
economy is going to save us out of this situation especially in the light of the trade
war which is being announced by the President of United States of America and I
am concluding with this.
So, I reasonably apprehend that the proportion of tariffs and taxation
proposals in the Finance Bill are not sufficient to boost our GDP growth and will
further affect the poor and the youths who are unemployed.
With these words, I conclude.
Thank you very much.

एफडीआई क(cid:236) सीमा 74 से बढ़ाकर 100 (cid:255)ितशत करने क(cid:236) बात कही है । (cid:179)या ऐसा िनण(cid:170)य भारत क(cid:236)
क(cid:204)पिनय(cid:332) के िहत(cid:332) के िलए उिचत होगा? िव(cid:176) म(cid:253)ं ी जी, मेरा एक सुझाव है िक देश म(cid:164) रा(cid:186)य(cid:332) क(cid:236)

SHRI VAMSI KRISHNA GADDAM (PEDDAPALLE): Madam, thank you very
much for this opportunity.
Madam, I rise today, not just as a representative, not just as a voice, but as
the echo of 20 crore dalits of this country. These dalit people have been
historically oppressed, systematically excluded, and even today, left at the bottom
of the economic pyramid despite all the tall claims, of Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas.
Year after year, the Government presents a Budget that claims to uplift this
24.03.2025 1112
marginalized community. But, I would like to ask where is this upliftment of Dalits
in these Budget numbers. Where is our rightful share? सबका साथ, सबका िवकास म (cid:164)
दिलत कह(cid:233) नह(cid:233) िदखता । बीजेपी क(cid:236) सरकार िसफ(cid:170) खुद का िवकास, दिलत(cid:332) का िवनाश कर रही ह ै ।
The Constitution of India, under Article 46, directs the State to promote the
educational and the economic interests of the Schedule Castes, and protect
them, from social injustice and exploitation. But, in the Budget, if you see, the
allocation towards SC welfare is hardly Rs. 1,65,000 crore, which is hardly 3.25
per cent of the entire Budget. When we are 16 per cent of the population, why are
we not getting what we deserve?
Madam, I would like to ask this House whether this is the idea of justice. Is
this the definition of development for all? We must remove all barriers for the
social and economic upliftment of SCs if we would like to progress as a nation.
This is what Dr. Ambedkar said. I demand a special tax concession for all dalit
entrepreneurs to help in the upliftment of this community.
Dalits have the lowest access to education and employment. Therefore,
we need to pay special attention towards them. We need specially linked
incentives for the dalits to help them improve their social skills development,
technical skills, and industrialization benefits to encourage the bottom-most
community. A special attention for the bottom-most community is much needed,
and for this we can refer the models of Karnataka and Telangana. A specific
attention must be given to dalit communities, like their colonies, and housing.
Madam, I come from the State of Telangana. We contribute a big chunk to
the GDP of our country. The State of Telangana and other Southern States
24.03.2025 1113
contribute 36 per cent of the GDP, but only 15 per cent is returned, and allocated
back to the Southern States. Why is this discrimination being done to the
Southern States of our country? Are we not part of Bharat? Are we not part of
‘Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas’? When Maha Kumbh happened, the concerned State
got special treatment in the form of special trains and railway lines for the
pilgrims. We have demanded special railway connections for pilgrims of Tirupati
and Sabarimala from my place Peddapalle, but nothing has been given.
Madam, the Government should, actually, give employee-linked incentives.
For corporates, instead of giving tax concessions, they should link it with creating
employment as 20 crore people of our country remain unemployed. I request the
Government to allocate Rs. 10,000 crore to Pedapalli, where I come from, in this
Budget. We need jobs there. We need to create textile parks in Chennur, and
Singareni to protect and create new jobs, green and clean technologies in NTPC
and Singareni, and start-up incubation hubs.
Madam, today, there are a lot of technological innovations happening.
Therefore, start-up incubation hubs for Pedapalli are required in this AI age. I
also request the Government to give tourism development grants for Dharmapuri
temple, and Ramagiri temples in Manthani. Apart from that, I also demand tax
exemptions for dalits and backward classes, and warehouse facilities for
Pedapalli. A new railway station infrastructure is also required for the
development of Pedapalli and Mancherial. We also need a BPO centre in
Pedapalli under India's BPO Scheme.
Madam with all this, I would also like to request this Government to pay
24.03.2025 1114
attention towards the dalits and do justice. We require justice and right allocation
of funds for Telangana and Pedapalli, in particular, because we all want to be a
part of this great initiative of ‘Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas’ of BJP.
Thank you.
SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): Madam, on behalf of the All India
Trinamool Congress, I rise to speak on the Finance Bill, 2025.
Madam, Aristotle known as the father of political science, had rightly said in
his book, Politics, that the best political community is the one, where the middle
class holds significant influence. But the plight of middle class in India is pitiful.
They are subjected to exorbitant taxes, even in times when they are facing issues
like, inadequate increment, unstable employment opportunity, and decreasing
employment generation. Moreover, the private companies are being forced to
shift to other locations like Singapore, Dubai, as they are harassed by the
Government for taxes and political donation.
Madam, our youth, the future of our nation, is facing employment crisis.
The Periodic Labour Force Survey showed youth unemployment rate at 10.2 per
cent for the year 2024. Also, as of January 2025, India's economic growth is
projected to slow to 6.4 per cent in this fiscal year. The dream of Viksit Bharat by
2047 are just empty words with such low labour force participation and jobless
growth. If educated graduates are not getting jobs, if the basic needs of people
are not being met, if more than half the people of our country live in abject
poverty, what reason does the Government have to gloat and pat itself on the
back?
24.03.2025 1115
Madam, it is no surprise that India's wealth inequality has reached obscene
level with the top one per cent controlling over 40.5 per cent of the nation's wealth
and the 10 richest individuals in the country hold a combined fortune that is
equivalent to 11.16 per cent of India's GDP. While the bottom 50 per cent barely
see any benefit despite this staggering concentration of wealth, India's tax system
remains regressive, where 64 per cent of GST comes from the bottom half of the
population, while the top 10 per cent contribute a meagre 4 per cent.
Madam, data from the Directorate General of GST Intelligence shows that
frauds amounting to Rs. 2 lakh crore were detected in 2023-24, which is a
significant increase from Rs. 1.1 lakh crore in the previous year. An estimated
2,30,000 small businesses have shut down due to complications arising from
GST compliances. GST mismanagement is a glaring issue that the Government
continuously fails to acknowledge and work on.
Madam, the hon. Prime Minister Modi ji, in 2024 election campaign, said
that ‘मिहलाओ ं को मंगलसू(cid:253) बेचना पड़ेगा ।‘ As per the RBI report in 2024-25, gold
mortgage loans stood at Rs. 1.2 lakh crore, while in 2018-19 the gold mortgage
loan was Rs. 25,000 crore. At the same time, NITI Aayog’s report claimed that out
of all the women taking loans by mortgaging their belongings in 2024-25, 38 per
cent were gold loans, which represent a 22 per cent increase compared to figures
from 2019. It clearly shows that the middle-class and lower income group are
undergoing a severe financial crisis.
It is not news that the Indian rupee is consistently weakening against the
US dollar. Our economic growth depends greatly upon trade as well as the
24.03.2025 1116
import-export policies. But the Government has failed to provide any clear
direction about how to strengthen our economy and the devaluing currency. …
(Interruptions)
Madam, we cannot deny the fact that India's investment climate has been
deteriorating under the current Government, despite grand claims of ‘Ease of
Doing Business’ and ‘Make in India’. We are actually struggling to attract
meaningful domestic and foreign investment. Private investment as a percentage
of GDP is stagnant at around 27 per cent, significantly lower than the 32 per cent
to 35 per cent during the previous decade. And if that is the case, how are we
going to move forward from here? We are missing out the opportunity to become
a global investment hub, and the current Government must take the responsibility
for this failure.
Madam, our economy is undergoing a severe crisis due to skyrocketing fuel
prices, food inflation and the rising cost of living. While this Government has
constantly blamed global factors for this problem, the real issues lie in poor
economic management, regulatory burden, excessive taxation and the lack of
proactive policy measures to control inflation. Ultimately, the burden of this falls
on the middle class. … (Interruptions)

ADV. GOWAAL KAGADA PADAVI (NANDURBAR): Thank you Madam
Chairperson. I just want to cover certain points and I will go straightforward into it.
The country has heard of tax-terrorism and now we have also seen the tax-
terrorists of this country, that is, this Government! The number of taxes that are
being levied are beyond the control of the Government and they do not have any
financial or economical strategy. They are kind of very clueless about this.
Today, there are nearly nine different rates of GST starting from zero per
cent to 28 per cent. We have a regime that has created one of the worst GSTs,
that is, the Goods and Services Tax that was supposed to be a simple tax. But in
the whole world, in about 50 countries, this is the worst tax collection system that
24.03.2025 1123
we have seen.
For the first time, the amount of money collected from companies is less
than the tax revenue collected from individual taxpayers, which is going up. The
corporate tax collection is up 1.2 per cent while the GST collection is up 12 per
cent, and income tax is up by 20 per cent. The economy is slowing down because
the Government is taking people's money leaving them with nothing to spend.
There is no growing demand. I feel that there is a need to mention that there
should be a cut in tax for the people and not in corporate tax.
I would also like to mention that the Congress had earlier proposed a GST
system having only three slabs. And now, there are three slabs for popcorn itself.
Caramelised popcorn has been taxed at 18 per cent. I will not go into the popcorn
fiasco because it was an explanation by the FM that had the country laughing.
एमएसएमई पर 18 परस(cid:164)ट जीएसटी लगा रहे ह(cid:167) on spare parts while larger firms
have to pay 12 per cent on finished products. I think that they should simplify it
and keep it at 15 per cent to promote the MSME business.
I would also like to mention that Malaysia, for instance, repealed its GST in
2018 due to public dissatisfaction and adverse effects on small and medium
businesses. I would also mention that the European Union's VAT system has
succeeded by incorporating tailored exemptions and subsidies to support smaller
enterprises. The Government should stop the freebies that they offer as this
ultimately puts burden on the Government's treasury. If the bureaucracy is active,
then they must also advise the Government that they should adopt good home
economic policies.
24.03.2025 1124
About 80 per cent of the Central Government's spending is funded through
taxes. This is much higher than countries like Brazil, Mexico, and China, which
depend less on tax collection.
The U.S. President Donald Trump has hinted at a potential scrapping of the
income tax system in America and replacing it with tariffs to fund the Government.
There is a plan to overhaul the US taxation system to shift the burden away from
American workers and businessmen. President Trump has been the champion of
the ‘America First’ policy. Will the Government also plan a ‘India First’ policy and
give benefits to the Indians?
The Prime Minister had adopted many of the Congress schemes, such as
the Apprenticeship Scheme. The Central Government must consider this change
in GST policy too. The Government must stop abusing its taxpayers. I would also
like to mention that the Union Finance Minister, Nirmala Sitharaman ji, said that
the income tax exemption of up to Rs. 12 lakh would put additional Rs. 1 lakh
crore in our hands. I think the perception has gained ground and this additional
cash would find its way into consumption increasing the demand in the Indian
economy.
The Indian household debt is at its all time high standing at 42.9 per cent of
GDP with Rs. 2.7 lakh crore outstanding credit debt in India. I would like to
mention that in 2018, the affordable houses sold were 54 per cent but in 2024,
the figure has come down to 26 per cent. Depressingly, luxury housing is
booming while the middle-class is struggling with affordability crisis.
सभापित महोदया, घर, जो एक करोड़ (cid:321)पये से (cid:186)यादा के थे, वे वष(cid:170) 2018 म(cid:164) 16 (cid:255)ितशत िबके

और वष(cid:170) 2024 म(cid:164) 43 (cid:255)ितशत िबके । इस तरह से लगभग तीन गनु ा (cid:186)यादा घर िबके । मतलब, अगर
आप एक करोड़ (cid:321)पये स े (cid:186)यादा कमाते ह(cid:167) तो आपक(cid:236) िजंदगी अ(cid:184)छी है, लेिकन अगर आप िमिडल या
लोअर िमिडल (cid:179)लास म (cid:164) आते ह (cid:167) और घर खरीदना चाहते है तो शायद आपका यह सपना, सपना ही
रह जाएगा । यह भी पता चल रहा है िक 2,000 घर(cid:332) का कं (cid:214)(cid:367)(cid:179)शन ठप पड़ा ह(cid:242)आ है और घर का
पजेशन िमलने म(cid:164) भी िमिनमम तीन स े चार साल(cid:332) का िडले होता है । In conclusion, I would just
like to say that रोटी, कपड़ा और मकान एक इमोशनल टैगलाइन नह(cid:233), बि(cid:208)क हक(cid:236)कत है, (cid:179)य(cid:332)िक
आज म(cid:167) संसद म(cid:164) एक एमपी के तौर पर नह(cid:233), बि(cid:208)क आम आदमी के डािकया के (cid:322)प म(cid:164) आया ह(cid:243) ँ ।
सभापित महोदया, मेरी आपके मा(cid:197)यम से यही कोिशश रहेगी िक आम आदमी क(cid:236) ज(cid:322)रत(cid:332) को

सभापित महोदया, म(cid:167) एक शेर सनु ाकर अपनी बात ख(cid:194)म क(cid:322)ं गा ।

LIVE TELECAST OF PROCEEDINGS OF LOK SABHA
Lok Sabha proceedings are being telecast live on Sansad T.V. Channel. Live telecast begins at 11
A.M. everyday the Lok Sabha sits, till the adjournment of the House.
